questions.subject: measure
questions.chapter: 1. Lebesgue Integral And Core Convergence Theorems
questions.questionHtml: State and prove the Monotone Convergence Theorem, or Beppo Levi Theorem.
questions.keyStepsHtml: \[ E_n=\{(1-\varepsilon)s\le f_n\},\qquad E_n\uparrow X,\qquad \int f_n\ge(1-\varepsilon)\int_{E_n}s. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Statement. Let \((X,\mathcal A,\mu)\) be a measure space. Let \((f_n)\subset M^+(X,\mathcal A)\) and suppose \(f_n\le f_{n+1}\) on \(X\) for every \(n\), and \(f_n\to f\) pointwise on \(X\), with \(f:X\to \overline{\mathbb R}_+\). Then \(f\in M^+(X,\mathcal A)\) and

\[ \lim_{n\to\infty}\int_X f_n\,d\mu=\int_X f\,d\mu=\int_X \lim_{n\to\infty} f_n\,d\mu. \]

Proof. Since \(f_n\le f\), monotonicity of the integral gives \(\int_X f_n d\mu\le \int_X f d\mu\). Hence \(\alpha:=\lim_n\int_X f_n d\mu\) exists in \([0,\infty]\) and \(\alpha\le \int_X f d\mu\).

For the converse, fix \(\varepsilon\in(0,1)\) and \(s\in S_f:=\{s\in S^+(X,\mathcal A):s\le f\}\). Define

\[ E_n:=\{x\in X:(1-\varepsilon)s(x)\le f_n(x)\}. \]

Then \(E_n\in\mathcal A\), \(E_n\subset E_{n+1}\), and \(\bigcup_n E_n=X\). Indeed, if \(f(x)<\infty\), then \((1-\varepsilon)s(x)\le(1-\varepsilon)f(x)

Therefore

\[ \int_X f_n d\mu\ge \int_{E_n} f_n d\mu\ge(1-\varepsilon)\int_{E_n}s\,d\mu. \]

Letting \(n\to\infty\) and using continuity from below for the measure associated with the simple function \(s\),

\[ \alpha\ge(1-\varepsilon)\lim_n\int_{E_n}s\,d\mu=(1-\varepsilon)\int_X s\,d\mu. \]

Letting \(\varepsilon\downarrow0\), \(\alpha\ge \int_Xs\,d\mu\). Taking the supremum over \(s\in S_f\) gives \(\alpha\ge\int_X f d\mu\). Hence equality holds.
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: measure
questions.chapter: 1. Lebesgue Integral And Core Convergence Theorems
questions.questionHtml: State and prove Fatou's Lemma.
questions.keyStepsHtml: \[ g_k=\inf_{m\ge k}f_m,\qquad g_k\uparrow \liminf_n f_n,\qquad g_k\le f_m\ (m\ge k). \]
questions.answerHtml:
Statement. Let \((f_n)\subset M^+(X,\mathcal A)\). Then

\[ \int_X \liminf_{n\to\infty} f_n\,d\mu\le \liminf_{n\to\infty}\int_X f_n\,d\mu. \]

Proof. For \(k\in\mathbb N\), set

\[ g_k:=\inf_{m\ge k} f_m. \]

Then \(g_k\in M^+(X,\mathcal A)\), \(g_k\le g_{k+1}\), and

\[ \lim_{k\to\infty}g_k=\sup_k\inf_{m\ge k}f_m=\liminf_{n\to\infty}f_n. \]

Moreover, for every \(m\ge k\), \(g_k\le f_m\), hence by monotonicity of the integral

\[ \int_Xg_k\,d\mu\le \int_Xf_m\,d\mu\qquad(m\ge k). \]

Taking the infimum over \(m\ge k\),

\[ \int_Xg_k\,d\mu\le \inf_{m\ge k}\int_Xf_m\,d\mu. \]

Now let \(k\to\infty\). Since \(g_k\uparrow\liminf_n f_n\), the Monotone Convergence Theorem gives

\[ \int_X \liminf_n f_n\,d\mu=\lim_k\int_Xg_k\,d\mu\le \lim_k\inf_{m\ge k}\int_X f_m\,d\mu=\liminf_n\int_X f_n\,d\mu. \]
questions.finalReason: repeated core ID

questions.subject: measure
questions.chapter: 1. Lebesgue Integral And Core Convergence Theorems
questions.questionHtml: State and prove the Lebesgue theorem.
questions.answerHtml:
Statement: Dominated Convergence Theorem. Let \((f_n)\subset M(X,\mathcal A)\), let \(f\in M(X,\mathcal A)\), and suppose \(f_n\to f\) a.e. in \(X\). Assume that there exists \(g\in L^1(X,\mathcal A,\mu)\), \(g\ge0\), such that

\[ |f_n|\le g\quad\text{a.e. in }X,\qquad \forall n. \]

Then \(f_n,f\in L^1(X)\),

\[ \int_X|f_n-f|\,d\mu\to0, \]

and in particular \(\int_X f_n d\mu\to\int_X f d\mu\).

Proof. Passing to the a.e. limit in \(|f_n|\le g\) gives \(|f|\le g\) a.e. Hence \(f_n,f\in L^1\). Also

\[ |f_n-f|\le |f_n|+|f|\le2g. \]

Set \(h_n:=2g-|f_n-f|\). Then \(h_n\in M^+(X,\mathcal A)\) and \(h_n\to2g\) a.e. By Fatou's Lemma,

\[ \int_X2g\,d\mu\le\liminf_n\int_Xh_n\,d\mu =\liminf_n\left(\int_X2g\,d\mu-\int_X|f_n-f|\,d\mu \right). \]

Since \(g\in L^1\), \(\int_X2g d\mu<\infty\), and therefore

\[ 0\le \limsup_n\int_X|f_n-f|\,d\mu\le0. \]

Thus \(\int_X|f_n-f|d\mu\to0\). Finally,

\[ \left|\int_Xf_n\,d\mu-\int_Xf\,d\mu\right|\le\int_X|f_n-f|\,d\mu\to0. \]
questions.finalReason: repeated core ID

questions.subject: measure
questions.chapter: 1. Lebesgue Integral And Core Convergence Theorems
questions.questionHtml: In which case is it simple to find a dominating function?
questions.answerHtml: A dominating function \(g \in L^1(X, \mu)\) is typically found using one of the following mathematical strategies: 1. Bounded Domains and Uniform Bounds: If \(\mu(X) < \infty\) and there exists a constant \(M > 0\) such that \(|f_n(x)| \le M\) a.e. for all \(n \ge 1\), we choose \(g(x) = M\). Since \(\int_X M \, d\mu = M \mu(X) < \infty\), \(g\) is integrable. 2. Pointwise Supremum / Decay Estimates: If the domain is unbounded (e.g., \(\mathbb{R}^d\)), we seek an integrable function that bounds \(|f_n(x)|\) uniformly in \(n\). - For example, if \(|f_n(x)| \le \frac{C}{1 + |x|^{d+\alpha}}\) for some \(\alpha > 0\), then \(g(x) = \frac{C}{1 + |x|^{d+\alpha}}\) is integrable on \(\mathbb{R}^d\). - If there is an integrable singularity, such as \(|f_n(x)| \le \frac{C}{|x|^\alpha}\) for \(\alpha < d\) on a bounded neighborhood of \(0\), we choose \(g(x) = \frac{C}{|x|^\alpha}\). 3. Monotonicity: If the sequence is bounded by a known integrable function \(|f_n(x)| \le h(x)\) a.e. for all \(n\), then \(g = h\) is the dominating function.
questions.finalReason: repeated core ID

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: For a sequence of functions \(\{f_n\}\in M\), write the definition of pointwise convergence.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Pointwise convergence is the fixed-point test: choose x first, then let n go to infinity.

A sequence of measurable functions \((f_n)\) converges pointwise to \(f\) on \(X\) if for every fixed \(x \in X\):

\[ \lim_{n\to\infty} f_n(x) = f(x). \]
Equivalently, for every \(x \in X\) and every \(\varepsilon > 0\), there exists an integer \(N = N(x, \varepsilon)\) such that \(|f_n(x) - f(x)| < \varepsilon\) for all \(n \ge N\). The threshold \(N\) may depend on the point \(x\).
questions.finalReason: repeated core ID

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: For a sequence of functions \(\{f_n\}\in M\), write the definition of almost everywhere convergence.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Almost everywhere convergence is pointwise convergence after throwing away one null set.

A sequence of measurable functions \((f_n)\) converges almost everywhere to \(f\) (denoted by \(f_n \to f\) a.e.) if there exists a measurable set \(N \in \mathcal{A}\) with \(\mu(N) = 0\) such that for all \(x \in X \setminus N\):

\[ \lim_{n\to\infty} f_n(x) = f(x). \]
This means that the sequence converges pointwise at every point outside of a negligible set.
questions.finalReason: repeated core ID

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: For a sequence of functions \(\{f_n\}\in M\), write the definition of convergence in measure.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Convergence in measure means every epsilon-bad set has measure tending to zero.

A sequence of measurable functions \((f_n)\) converges in measure to \(f\) if for every \(\varepsilon > 0\):

\[ \lim_{n\to\infty} \mu(\{ x \in X : |f_n(x) - f(x)| > \varepsilon \}) = 0. \]
This means that the measure of the set where the function values deviate by more than \(\varepsilon\) tends to zero as \(n \to \infty\).
questions.finalReason: repeated core ID

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: For a sequence of functions \(\{f_n\}\in M\), write the definition of convergence in \(L^1\).
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Convergence in L1 means the integral of the absolute error tends to zero.

A sequence of functions \((f_n)\) in \(L^1(X, \mu)\) converges in \(L^1\) (or converges in norm) to \(f \in L^1(X, \mu)\) if:

\[ \lim_{n\to\infty} \|f_n - f\|_{L^1} = \lim_{n\to\infty} \int_X |f_n - f| \, d\mu = 0. \]
questions.finalReason: repeated core ID

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Under which hypothesis on \(X\) does convergence a.e. imply convergence in measure?
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ E_n = \bigcup_{k=n}^\infty \{ x \in X : |f_k(x) - f(x)| > \varepsilon \}. \]
\[ \lim_{n\to\infty} \mu(E_n) = \mu(E) = 0. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: The missing hypothesis is finite total measure; use Egorov-style continuity from above.

The hypothesis required is that the measure space is finite, i.e., \(\mu(X) < \infty\). - Proof: Suppose \(f_n \to f\) a.e. Let \(S \subset X\) be the set where \(f_n(x) \to f(x)\), so \(\mu(X \setminus S) = 0\). Fix any \(\varepsilon > 0\). For each \(n \ge 1\), define:

\[ E_n = \bigcup_{k=n}^\infty \{ x \in X : |f_k(x) - f(x)| > \varepsilon \}. \]
The sets \(E_n\) are nested and decreasing: \(E_{n+1} \subset E_n\). Their intersection is \(E = \bigcap_{n=1}^\infty E_n\). If \(x \in E\), then \(|f_k(x) - f(x)| > \varepsilon\) for infinitely many \(k\), which means \(f_n(x) \not\to f(x)\). Thus, \(E \subset X \setminus S\), which implies \(\mu(E) = 0\). Since \(\mu(X) < \infty\), we have \(\mu(E_1) \le \mu(X) < \infty\). Applying the continuity of measure from above:
\[ \lim_{n\to\infty} \mu(E_n) = \mu(E) = 0. \]
Since \(\{ x \in X : |f_n(x) - f(x)| > \varepsilon \} \subset E_n\), by monotonicity:
\[ \mu(\{ x \in X : |f_n(x) - f(x)| > \varepsilon \}) \le \mu(E_n) \to 0 \quad \text{as } n \to \infty, \]
proving convergence in measure.

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: What is the counterexample when \(\mu(X) = \infty\)?
questions.strategyHtml: The problem wants a sharp yes/no answer, not just a slogan. Decide the claimed implication, then either prove it from the definitions or choose a standard model example, verify the stated convergence/property, and explicitly show the missing conclusion fails.
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ f_n = \chi_{[n, n+1]}. \]
\[ \lambda(\{ x \in \mathbb{R} : |f_n(x)| > \varepsilon \}) = \lambda([n, n+1]) = 1. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: On infinite measure, escaping mass such as chi_[n,n+1] converges pointwise but not in measure.

Without the finite-measure hypothesis, almost everywhere convergence does not imply convergence in measure. - Counterexample: Consider \(\mathbb{R}\) equipped with Lebesgue measure \(\lambda\). Define the sequence of functions \((f_n)_{n=1}^\infty\) by:

\[ f_n = \chi_{[n, n+1]}. \]
For any fixed \(x \in \mathbb{R}\), choosing \(n > x\) implies \(x \notin [n, n+1]\), so \(f_n(x) = 0\) for all \(n > x\). Thus, \(f_n \to 0\) pointwise everywhere on \(\mathbb{R}\). However, for any \(\varepsilon \in (0, 1)\) and any \(n \ge 1\), the set where \(|f_n| > \varepsilon\) is \([n, n+1]\), which has measure:
\[ \lambda(\{ x \in \mathbb{R} : |f_n(x)| > \varepsilon \}) = \lambda([n, n+1]) = 1. \]
Since this measure does not converge to \(0\) as \(n \to \infty\), the sequence does not converge to \(0\) in measure.
questions.finalReason: July exact history

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: What is the relation between convergence in measure and convergence a.e. up to subsequences?
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Convergence in measure is exactly a.e. convergence after passing to a suitable subsequence.

If \(f_n \to f\) in measure, then there exists a subsequence \((f_{n_k})\) that converges to \(f\) almost everywhere. - Subsequence Construction: Since \(f_n \to f\) in measure, we can select a subsequence \((f_{n_k})\) such that for each \(k \ge 1\), the set \(A_k = \{ x \in X : |f_{n_k}(x) - f(x)| > 2^{-k} \}\) satisfies \(\mu(A_k) < 2^{-k}\). - Borel-Cantelli Argument: The sum of the measures of these sets is finite: \(\sum_{k=1}^\infty \mu(A_k) < \sum_{k=1}^\infty 2^{-k} = 1 < \infty\). By the First Borel-Cantelli Lemma, the set \(E = \limsup_{k\to\infty} A_k = \bigcap_{N=1}^\infty \bigcup_{k=N}^\infty A_k\) of points belonging to infinitely many \(A_k\) has measure \(\mu(E) = 0\). For any \(x \notin E\), there exists \(K(x)\) such that for all \(k \ge K(x)\), \(x \notin A_k\), which means \(|f_{n_k}(x) - f(x)| \le 2^{-k}\). Since \(2^{-k} \to 0\), \(\lim_{k\to\infty} f_{n_k}(x) = f(x)\) for all \(x \notin E\). Since \(\mu(E) = 0\), \(f_{n_k} \to f\) a.e.
questions.finalReason: not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Show that convergence in \(L^1\) implies convergence in measure.
questions.strategyHtml: The wanted implication is an estimate between two notions of convergence. Fix a tolerance, convert a large-deviation set into an integral bound with Chebyshev/Markov, then send the \(L^1\) norm to zero.
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ \mu(\{ x \in X : |f_n(x) - f(x)| > \varepsilon \}) \le \frac{1}{\varepsilon} \int_X |f_n - f| \, d\mu. \]
\[ \lim_{n\to\infty} \mu(\{ x \in X : |f_n(x) - f(x)| > \varepsilon \}) \le \frac{1}{\varepsilon} \cdot 0 = 0. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Use Chebyshev: the epsilon-bad set is controlled by the L1 norm divided by epsilon.

Assume \(f_n \to f\) in \(L^1(X, \mu)\). Fix any \(\varepsilon > 0\). By Chebyshev's inequality (or Markov's inequality), we have:

\[ \mu(\{ x \in X : |f_n(x) - f(x)| > \varepsilon \}) \le \frac{1}{\varepsilon} \int_X |f_n - f| \, d\mu. \]
Since \(f_n \to f\) in \(L^1\), the integral on the right-hand side tends to \(0\) as \(n \to \infty\). Since \(\varepsilon > 0\) is a fixed constant, we have:
\[ \lim_{n\to\infty} \mu(\{ x \in X : |f_n(x) - f(x)| > \varepsilon \}) \le \frac{1}{\varepsilon} \cdot 0 = 0. \]
Thus, \(f_n \to f\) in measure.
questions.finalReason: repeated core ID

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Show that convergence in \(L^1\) implies convergence a.e. up to subsequences.
questions.strategyHtml: The proof should build a fast subsequence. Pick indices so the bad sets have summable measures, apply Borel-Cantelli, and conclude that outside one null set the errors eventually stay below a summable threshold.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: From L1 convergence choose a fast subsequence, sum the errors, and apply Borel-Cantelli style reasoning.

Let \(f_n \to f\) in \(L^1(X, \mu)\). 1. By Chebyshev's inequality, for any \(\varepsilon > 0\), we have \(\mu(\{|f_n - f| > \varepsilon\}) \le \frac{1}{\varepsilon} \|f_n - f\|_{L^1} \to 0\). Thus, \(f_n \to f\) in measure. 2. We extract a subsequence \((f_{n_k})\) such that for each \(k \ge 1\), the set \(A_k = \{ x \in X : |f_{n_k}(x) - f(x)| > 2^{-k} \}\) satisfies \(\mu(A_k) < 2^{-k}\). 3. Since \(\sum_{k=1}^\infty \mu(A_k) < \sum_{k=1}^\infty 2^{-k} = 1 < \infty\), the First Borel-Cantelli Lemma implies that the set \(E = \limsup_{k\to\infty} A_k\) of points belonging to infinitely many \(A_k\) has measure \(\mu(E) = 0\). 4. For any \(x \notin E\), there exists \(K(x)\) such that for all \(k \ge K(x)\), we have \(x \notin A_k\), which means \(|f_{n_k}(x) - f(x)| \le 2^{-k}\). 5. Since \(2^{-k} \to 0\), we have \(\lim_{k\to\infty} f_{n_k}(x) = f(x)\) for all \(x \notin E\), which proves \(f_{n_k} \to f\) a.e.
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Does convergence in measure imply convergence in \(L^1\)? Justify the answer.
questions.strategyHtml: The problem wants a sharp yes/no answer, not just a slogan. Decide the claimed implication, then either prove it from the definitions or choose a standard model example, verify the stated convergence/property, and explicitly show the missing conclusion fails.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: In measure does not control mass size; use a spike n chi_(0,1/n).

No, convergence in measure does not imply convergence in \(L^1\). - Justification: Consider the interval \(X = (0, 1)\) with Lebesgue measure. Define the sequence of functions \((f_n)_{n=1}^\infty\) by:

\[ f_n = n \chi_{(0, 1/n)}. \]
For any \(\varepsilon > 0\), if \(n > 1/\varepsilon\), then \(\{x \in (0,1) : |f_n(x)| > \varepsilon\} = (0, 1/n)\). The measure is:
\[ \lambda(\{x \in (0,1) : |f_n(x)| > \varepsilon\}) = \frac{1}{n} \to 0 \quad \text{as } n \to \infty. \]
Thus, \(f_n \to 0\) in measure. However, the \(L^1\) norm of each \(f_n\) is:
\[ \|f_n\|_{L^1} = \int_0^1 n \chi_{(0, 1/n)}(x) \, dx = n \cdot \frac{1}{n} = 1. \]
Since \(\|f_n - 0\|_{L^1} = 1 \not\to 0\), the sequence does not converge to \(0\) in \(L^1\).
questions.finalReason: July exact history; repeated core ID

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Does convergence a.e. imply convergence in \(L^1\)? Justify the answer.
questions.strategyHtml: The problem wants a sharp yes/no answer, not just a slogan. Decide the claimed implication, then either prove it from the definitions or choose a standard model example, verify the stated convergence/property, and explicitly show the missing conclusion fails.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: A.e. convergence does not control integrals without domination or uniform integrability.

No, almost everywhere convergence does not imply convergence in \(L^1\). - Justification: Consider the interval \(X = (0, 1)\) with Lebesgue measure. Let:

\[ f_n = n \chi_{(0, 1/n)}. \]
For any \(x \in (0, 1)\), choosing \(n > 1/x\) implies \(x \notin (0, 1/n)\), so \(f_n(x) = 0\) for all \(n > 1/x\). Thus, \(f_n \to 0\) pointwise everywhere on \((0, 1)\). However, as calculated above, \(\|f_n\|_{L^1} = 1\) for all \(n \ge 1\). Thus, \(\|f_n - 0\|_{L^1} = 1 \not\to 0\), meaning the sequence does not converge to \(0\) in \(L^1\). Almost everywhere convergence only guarantees \(L^1\) convergence under additional conditions, such as domination by an integrable function (Lebesgue Dominated Convergence Theorem).
questions.finalReason: July exact history; repeated core ID

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Does convergence in \(L^1\) imply a.e. convergence? Justify the answer.
questions.strategyHtml: The problem wants a sharp yes/no answer, not just a slogan. Decide the claimed implication, then either prove it from the definitions or choose a standard model example, verify the stated convergence/property, and explicitly show the missing conclusion fails.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: \(L^1\) convergence gives convergence in measure, hence an a.e. convergent subsequence, but not necessarily a.e. convergence of the whole sequence.

No, convergence in \(L^1\) does not imply almost everywhere convergence of the full sequence. What is true is the subsequence statement:

\[ f_n\to f\text{ in }L^1(X) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \exists f_{n_k}: f_{n_k}\to f\text{ a.e. in }X. \]

Step 1: \(L^1\) convergence implies convergence in measure. Let \(\varepsilon>0\). By Chebyshev's inequality,

\[ \mu(\{|f_n-f|\ge\varepsilon\})\le {1\over\varepsilon}\int_X |f_n-f|\,d\mu. \]

Since \(\int_X |f_n-f|d\mu\to0\), the right hand side tends to \(0\), so \(f_n\to f\) in measure.

Step 2: convergence in measure gives an a.e. convergent subsequence. Apply the standard theorem: if \(f_n,f\) are finite a.e. and \(f_n\to f\) in measure, then there is a subsequence \((f_{n_k})\) such that \(f_{n_k}\to f\) a.e. Since \(L^1\) functions are finite a.e., the hypotheses are satisfied.

The full sequence need not converge a.e.; the standard typewriter sequence on \([0,1]\) satisfies \(f_n\to0\) in \(L^1\), but it does not converge pointwise anywhere.
questions.finalReason: July exact history

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Does convergence in measure imply a.e. convergence? Justify the answer.
questions.strategyHtml: The problem wants a sharp yes/no answer, not just a slogan. Decide the claimed implication, then either prove it from the definitions or choose a standard model example, verify the stated convergence/property, and explicitly show the missing conclusion fails.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: From in measure, choose errors \(2^{-k}\) with exceptional sets of measure \(<2^{-k}\), then use tail unions.

No, convergence in measure does not imply a.e. convergence of the full sequence. It implies a.e. convergence only up to a subsequence.

Theorem. Let \(f_n,f\in M(X,\mathcal A)\) be finite a.e. If \(f_n\to f\) in measure, then there exists a subsequence \((f_{n_k})\) such that \(f_{n_k}\to f\) a.e.

Proof. Since \(f_n\to f\) in measure, for every \(k\ge1\) we can choose \(n_k>n_{k-1}\) such that

\[ E_k:=\{x\in X: |f_{n_k}(x)-f(x)|\ge 2^{-k}\},\qquad \mu(E_k)<2^{-k}. \]

Define the tail sets

\[ F_m:=\bigcup_{k=m}^{\infty}E_k. \]

By subadditivity,

\[ \mu(F_m)\le \sum_{k=m}^{\infty}\mu(E_k)<\sum_{k=m}^{\infty}2^{-k}=2^{1-m}. \]

Let \(E:=\bigcap_{m=1}^{\infty}F_m\), the set of points belonging to infinitely many \(E_k\). Since \((F_m)\) is decreasing and \(\mu(F_1)<\infty\), continuity from above gives

\[ \mu(E)=\lim_{m\to\infty}\mu(F_m)=0. \]

If \(x\notin E\), then for some \(m\), \(x\notin F_m\), hence \(x\notin E_k\) for all \(k\ge m\). Therefore

\[ |f_{n_k}(x)-f(x)|<2^{-k}\qquad(k\ge m), \]

and \(f_{n_k}(x)\to f(x)\). Thus \(f_{n_k}\to f\) a.e. The full sequence can still fail to converge a.e.; the typewriter sequence on \([0,1]\) is the standard counterexample.
questions.finalReason: July exact history

questions.subject: measure
questions.chapter: 3. Lebesgue Points And Fundamental Theorems Of Calculus
questions.questionHtml: Write the definition of Lebesgue point.
questions.strategyHtml: Use the one-dimensional interval-average definition used in the lecture notes. The essential point is average oscillation around the value at the point, not pointwise continuity.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Lebesgue point means vanishing average oscillation: subtract f(x), integrate on [x,x+h], divide by |h|.

Let \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb R\) be measurable, and let \(x_0\in[a,b]\) be a point at which \(f(x_0)\) is defined. We say that \(x_0\) is a Lebesgue point of \(f\) if

\[ \lim_{h\to0}\frac1{|h|}\int_{x_0}^{x_0+h}|f(t)-f(x_0)|\,d\lambda(t)=0, \]

where \(h\neq0\) and \(x_0+h\in[a,b]\). Equivalently, the mean oscillation of \(f\) around \(x_0\) tends to zero as the interval shrinks to \(x_0\). A continuity point is a Lebesgue point, but the definition is weaker: it only controls the integral average.
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: measure
questions.chapter: 3. Lebesgue Points And Fundamental Theorems Of Calculus
questions.questionHtml: What is about the measure of the set of points that are not Lebesgue points for a function \(f\in L^1\)?
questions.strategyHtml: State the Lebesgue differentiation theorem in the form needed for the exam. The conclusion is about a null exceptional set.
questions.keyStepsHtml: \[ f\in L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}((a,b))\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \lambda(\{x: x\text{ is not a Lebesgue point of }f\})=0. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Lebesgue point means vanishing average oscillation: subtract f(x), integrate on [x,x+h], divide by |h|.

If \(f\in L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}((a,b))\), then almost every \(x_0\in(a,b)\) is a Lebesgue point of \(f\). Therefore

\[ \lambda\bigl(\{x_0\in(a,b):x_0\text{ is not a Lebesgue point of }f\}\bigr)=0. \]

In particular, for \(f\in L^1((a,b))\), the set where the defining limit fails, or fails to be zero, is negligible. This is the result used to recover an \(L^1\) function almost everywhere from its integral primitive.
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: measure
questions.chapter: 3. Lebesgue Points And Fundamental Theorems Of Calculus
questions.questionHtml: State and prove the First Fundamental Theorem of Calculus for \(f\in L^1\).
questions.strategyHtml: Define the primitive \(F(x)=\int_{[a,x]}f\,d\lambda\). At a Lebesgue point, subtract \(f(x)\) from the difference quotient and estimate by the mean oscillation.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: At a Lebesgue point, the primitive difference quotient is exactly the average of f(t)-f(x).

Theorem. Let \(f\in L^1((a,b))\), and define

\[ F(x):=\int_{[a,x]} f\,d\lambda,\qquad x\in[a,b]. \]

Then \(F\) is differentiable for a.e. \(x\in(a,b)\), and

\[ F'(x)=f(x)\qquad\text{for a.e. }x\in(a,b). \]

Proof. Let \(x\in(a,b)\) be a Lebesgue point of \(f\), and take \(h\neq0\) with \(x+h\in[a,b]\). By linearity of the integral,

\[ \frac{F(x+h)-F(x)}h-f(x) =\frac1h\int_x^{x+h}(f(t)-f(x))\,dt. \]

Hence, by the triangle inequality,

\[ \left|\frac{F(x+h)-F(x)}h-f(x)\right| \le \frac1{|h|}\int_x^{x+h}|f(t)-f(x)|\,dt. \]

Since \(x\) is a Lebesgue point, the right-hand side tends to \(0\) as \(h\to0\). Therefore \(F'(x)=f(x)\) at every Lebesgue point of \(f\). Since almost every point is a Lebesgue point for functions in \(L^1((a,b))\), the conclusion holds a.e. in \((a,b)\).
questions.finalReason: July exact history; repeated core ID

questions.subject: measure
questions.chapter: 3. Lebesgue Points And Fundamental Theorems Of Calculus
questions.questionHtml: State the Second Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
questions.strategyHtml: State the exact equivalence: absolute continuity is equivalent to a.e. differentiability, integrable derivative, and reconstruction by the integral.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: The FTC bridge is: almost every point is Lebesgue, so the integral primitive differentiates back to f a.e.

Theorem: Second Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. Let \(\varphi:[a,b]\to\mathbb R\). Then the following are equivalent:

  1. \(\varphi\in AC([a,b])\).
  2. \(\varphi\) is differentiable a.e. in \([a,b]\), \(\varphi'\in L^1([a,b])\), and for every \(x\in[a,b]\),
\[ \varphi(x)-\varphi(a)=\int_{[a,x]}\varphi'\,d\lambda. \]

Thus absolute continuity is precisely the condition under which a function can be recovered everywhere from its a.e. derivative by the Lebesgue integral.
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: measure
questions.chapter: 4. Absolutely Continuous Functions And Sobolev Spaces
questions.questionHtml: Write the definition of absolutely continuous function.
questions.strategyHtml: Give the finite-family interval definition. The important quantifier is: every finite family of intervals with small total length has small total variation.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: AC controls total oscillation on finitely many disjoint intervals, not just one interval.

Let \(J=[a,b]\), and let \(f:J\to\mathbb R\). We say that \(f\) is absolutely continuous on \(J\), and write \(f\in AC([a,b])\), if for every \(\varepsilon>0\) there exists \(\delta>0\) such that for every finite family of intervals \(\{[a_k,b_k]\}_{k=1}^n\subset J\) with pairwise disjoint interiors,

\[ \sum_{k=1}^n(b_k-a_k)<\delta \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \sum_{k=1}^n|f(b_k)-f(a_k)|<\varepsilon. \]

The definition is stronger than uniform continuity because it controls the total oscillation of \(f\) on many disjoint intervals at once.
questions.finalReason: July exact history; repeated core ID

questions.subject: measure
questions.chapter: 4. Absolutely Continuous Functions And Sobolev Spaces
questions.questionHtml: Write the definition of weak derivative.
questions.keyStepsHtml: \[ \int_a^b u(x) \varphi'(x) \, dx = - \int_a^b v(x) \varphi(x) \, dx \qquad \forall \varphi \in C_c^1(a, b). \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Weak derivative means move the derivative onto the test function and pay the minus sign.

Let \(u \in L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}(a, b)\). A function \(v \in L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}(a, b)\) is the weak derivative of \(u\) (written \(u' = v\)) if:

\[ \int_a^b u(x) \varphi'(x) \, dx = - \int_a^b v(x) \varphi(x) \, dx \qquad \forall \varphi \in C_c^1(a, b). \]
If such a function \(v\) exists, it is unique up to equality almost everywhere.
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: measure
questions.chapter: 4. Absolutely Continuous Functions And Sobolev Spaces
questions.questionHtml: Write the definition of \(W^{1,p}\).
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ W^{1, p}(a, b) = \{ u \in L^p(a, b) : u \text{ has a weak derivative } u' \in L^p(a, b) \}. \]
\[ \|u\|_{W^{1, p}} = \left( \|u\|_{L^p}^p + \|u'\|_{L^p}^p \right)^{1/p} \quad (1 \le p < \infty), \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: For AC questions, remember: u(x)=u(a)+integral of an L1 derivative.

For \(1 \le p \le \infty\), the Sobolev space \(W^{1, p}(a, b)\) is defined as:

\[ W^{1, p}(a, b) = \{ u \in L^p(a, b) : u \text{ has a weak derivative } u' \in L^p(a, b) \}. \]
It is equipped with the norm:
\[ \|u\|_{W^{1, p}} = \left( \|u\|_{L^p}^p + \|u'\|_{L^p}^p \right)^{1/p} \quad (1 \le p < \infty), \]
and \(\|u\|_{W^{1, \infty}} = \max \{ \|u\|_{L^\infty}, \, \|u'\|_{L^\infty} \}\) for \(p = \infty\).
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: measure
questions.chapter: 4. Absolutely Continuous Functions And Sobolev Spaces
questions.questionHtml: State and prove the characterization of absolutely continuous functions in terms of the integration by parts formula.
questions.strategyHtml: Use test functions to express the derivative weakly. For an AC function, integration by parts gives the identity; conversely the identity gives an \(L^1\) weak derivative and hence an AC representative.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: AC controls total oscillation on finitely many disjoint intervals, not just one interval.

A function \(u\) is absolutely continuous on \([a,b]\) if and only if \(u\) has an \(L^1\)-derivative in the weak sense. More precisely, \(u\in AC([a,b])\) if and only if \(u\in C^0([a,b])\), \(u\) is differentiable a.e., \(u'\in L^1([a,b])\), and

\[ \int_a^b u'(x)\varphi(x)\,dx=-\int_a^b u(x)\varphi'(x)\,dx \qquad\forall\varphi\in C_c^\infty((a,b)). \]

If \(u\in AC([a,b])\), then \(u'\in L^1\) and the formula follows from integration by parts; the boundary term vanishes because \(\varphi\) has compact support in \((a,b)\). Conversely, if such a function \(v\in L^1\) satisfies

\[ \int_a^b v\varphi=-\int_a^b u\varphi' \qquad\forall\varphi\in C_c^\infty((a,b)), \]

then \(v\) is the weak derivative of \(u\). Define \(z(x)=\int_a^x v(t)\,dt\). Then \(z\in AC([a,b])\), and the distributional derivative of \(u-z\) is zero. Hence \(u-z\) is a.e. constant, so \(u\) has an absolutely continuous representative. In that representative, the weak derivative and the classical a.e. derivative coincide.
questions.finalReason: July exact history; repeated core ID

questions.subject: measure
questions.chapter: 4. Absolutely Continuous Functions And Sobolev Spaces
questions.questionHtml: State another characterization of absolutely continuous functions in terms of \(W^{1,1}\) and AC.
questions.strategyHtml: State the structural characterization: AC functions are exactly primitives of \(L^1\) functions. This is the bridge to \(W^{1,1}\).
questions.keyStepsHtml: \[ u\in AC([a,b])\iff \exists g\in L^1([a,b])\text{ such that }u(x)=u(a)+\int_a^xg(t)\,dt. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: AC controls total oscillation on finitely many disjoint intervals, not just one interval.

Characterization. A function \(u:[a,b]\to\mathbb R\) is absolutely continuous if and only if there exists \(g\in L^1([a,b])\) such that

\[ u(x)=u(a)+\int_a^x g(t)\,dt\qquad\forall x\in[a,b]. \]

In this case \(u\) is differentiable a.e. and \(u'=g\) a.e. Conversely, if \(u\in AC([a,b])\), then its a.e. derivative belongs to \(L^1([a,b])\), and the formula holds with \(g=u'\).

This is the same content as the second Fundamental Theorem of Calculus: absolutely continuous functions are exactly the indefinite integrals of \(L^1\) functions, up to the initial value \(u(a)\).
questions.finalReason: July exact history; repeated core ID

questions.subject: measure
questions.chapter: 4. Absolutely Continuous Functions And Sobolev Spaces
questions.questionHtml: What is the relation between \(W^{1,1}\) and AC? Justify the answer.
questions.strategyHtml: Be precise about representatives: \(W^{1,1}\) is made of a.e. equivalence classes, while \(AC\) contains pointwise continuous functions.
questions.keyStepsHtml: \[ W^{1,1}([a,b])=AC([a,b])\quad\text{after choosing the unique AC representative.} \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: W11 is AC after choosing the correct representative; equivalence classes hide point values.

The precise relation is:

\[ W^{1,1}([a,b])\equiv AC([a,b]) \]

in the following sense. If \(u\in AC([a,b])\), then its equivalence class belongs to \(W^{1,1}([a,b])\), and its classical derivative a.e. is its weak derivative. Conversely, if an equivalence class \(u\in W^{1,1}([a,b])\), then it has a unique absolutely continuous representative.

Indeed, let \(u\in W^{1,1}([a,b])\), and let \(w\in L^1([a,b])\) be its weak derivative. Define

\[ z(x):=\int_a^x w(t)\,dt. \]

Then \(z\in AC([a,b])\). For every \(\varphi\in C_c^\infty((a,b))\), using the definition of weak derivative and integration by parts for \(z\),

\[ \int_a^b (z-u)\varphi'\,dx=0. \]

Hence \(z-u\) is a.e. constant. Therefore \(u\) is equal a.e. to \(z-c\), which is absolutely continuous. This gives the AC representative. It is unique because two continuous functions that agree a.e. on \([a,b]\) agree everywhere.
questions.finalReason: July exact history; repeated core ID

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Write the definition of measurable function. State the theorem about the measurability of the composite function.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Compositions work when the middle sigma algebra matches the inverse-image test.

Let \((X, \mathcal{A})\) and \((Y, \mathcal{B})\) be measurable spaces. A function \(f : X \to Y\) is measurable if:

\[ f^{-1}(B) \in \mathcal{A} \qquad \forall B \in \mathcal{B}. \]
- Theorem (Composition): If \(f : (X, \mathcal{A}) \to (Y, \mathcal{B})\) is measurable and \(g : (Y, \mathcal{B}) \to (Z, \mathcal{C})\) is measurable, then their composition \(g \circ f : (X, \mathcal{A}) \to (Z, \mathcal{C})\) is measurable. Proof: For any \(C \in \mathcal{C}\), we have \((g \circ f)^{-1}(C) = f^{-1}(g^{-1}(C))\). Since \(g\) is measurable, \(g^{-1}(C) \in \mathcal{B}\). Since \(f\) is measurable, \(f^{-1}(g^{-1}(C)) \in \mathcal{A}\). - Critical Warning: If \(f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) and \(g : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) are Lebesgue measurable, their composition \(g \circ f\) is not necessarily Lebesgue measurable. This is because \(g^{-1}(C)\) for a Borel set \(C\) is Lebesgue measurable but not necessarily Borel, and \(f\) is only guaranteed to pull back Borel sets to Lebesgue measurable sets. However, if \(g\) is Borel measurable and \(f\) is Lebesgue measurable, then \(g \circ f\) is Lebesgue measurable.
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Write the definitions of Borel measurable functions and Lebesgue measurable functions. Prove that continuous functions are both Borel and Lebesgue measurable.
questions.strategyHtml: The proof wants measurability reduced to preimages or level sets. Rewrite the target function using countable unions/intersections and known measurable building blocks, then use closure of the sigma-algebra.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Compositions work when the middle sigma algebra matches the inverse-image test.

Let \(f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) be a function. 1. Borel Measurable: \(f\) is Borel measurable if for every Borel set \(B \in \mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})\), the pullback \(f^{-1}(B)\) is a Borel set: \(f^{-1}(B) \in \mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})\). 2. Lebesgue Measurable: \(f\) is Lebesgue measurable if for every Borel set \(B \in \mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})\), the pullback \(f^{-1}(B)\) is a Lebesgue measurable set: \(f^{-1}(B) \in \mathcal{L}(\mathbb{R})\).

Proof for Continuous Functions: Let \(f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) be continuous. Let \(O \subset \mathbb{R}\) be an open set. Since \(f\) is continuous, the inverse image \(f^{-1}(O)\) is open in \(\mathbb{R}\). Since every open set is a Borel set, \(f^{-1}(O) \in \mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})\). The family of sets \(S = \{ A \subset \mathbb{R} : f^{-1}(A) \in \mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R}) \}\) forms a \(\sigma\)-algebra. Since \(S\) contains all open sets, it contains the \(\sigma\)-algebra generated by open sets, which is \(\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})\). Thus, for every \(B \in \mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})\), \(f^{-1}(B) \in \mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})\), proving \(f\) is Borel measurable. Furthermore, since \(\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R}) \subset \mathcal{L}(\mathbb{R})\), we also have \(f^{-1}(B) \in \mathcal{L}(\mathbb{R})\) for all Borel sets \(B\), proving \(f\) is Lebesgue measurable.
questions.finalReason: July exact history

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Write the characterization of measurability of functions.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Measurability is tested by level sets; rays generate the Borel sigma algebra.

For a real-valued function \(f : X \to \mathbb{R}\) on a measurable space \((X, \mathcal{A})\), the following statements are equivalent: 1. \(f\) is measurable. 2. \(\{ x \in X : f(x) > a \} \in \mathcal{A}\) for all \(a \in \mathbb{R}\). 3. \(\{ x \in X : f(x) \ge a \} \in \mathcal{A}\) for all \(a \in \mathbb{R}\). 4. \(\{ x \in X : f(x) < a \} \in \mathcal{A}\) for all \(a \in \mathbb{R}\). 5. \(\{ x \in X : f(x) \le a \} \in \mathcal{A}\) for all \(a \in \mathbb{R}\). It suffices to verify these inequalities for all rational numbers \(a \in \mathbb{Q}\), which forms a countable dense subset of \(\mathbb{R}\).
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Establish all equivalent statements to the fact that \(f:X\to\mathbb R\) is measurable.
questions.keyStepsHtml: \[ \{f > a\} \in \mathcal{A}, \quad \{f \ge a\} \in \mathcal{A}, \quad \{f < a\} \in \mathcal{A}, \quad \{f \le a\} \in \mathcal{A} \qquad \forall a \in \mathbb{R} \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Measurability is tested by level sets; rays generate the Borel sigma algebra.

For \(f : X \to \mathbb{R}\), the equivalence of measurability to the conditions:

\[ \{f > a\} \in \mathcal{A}, \quad \{f \ge a\} \in \mathcal{A}, \quad \{f < a\} \in \mathcal{A}, \quad \{f \le a\} \in \mathcal{A} \qquad \forall a \in \mathbb{R} \]
follows from the properties of \(\sigma\)-algebras under complements and countable unions: - \(\{f \le a\} = X \setminus \{f > a\}\). - \(\{f \ge a\} = \bigcap_{n=1}^\infty \{f > a - 1/n\}\). - \(\{f < a\} = X \setminus \{f \ge a\}\). - \(\{f > a\} = \bigcup_{n=1}^\infty \{f \ge a + 1/n\}\).
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Let \(f,g\in M(X,\mathcal A)\). What can we say about measurability of \(\{f<g\},\{f\le g\},\{f=g\}\)?
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: For measurable-function questions, translate every operation into inverse images of Borel sets.

All three sets are measurable. Since \(f\) and \(g\) are real-valued measurable functions, their difference \(h = f - g\) is also a measurable function. We can express the sets as: - \(\{f < g\} = \{x \in X : h(x) < 0\} = h^{-1}((-\infty, 0)) \in \mathcal{A}\). - \(\{f \le g\} = \{x \in X : h(x) \le 0\} = h^{-1}((-\infty, 0]) \in \mathcal{A}\). - \(\{f = g\} = \{x \in X : h(x) = 0\} = h^{-1}(\{0\}) \in \mathcal{A}\). Since each of these interval generators is a Borel set, their pullbacks belong to \(\mathcal{A}\).
questions.finalReason: not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Let \(\{f_n\}\subset M(X,\mathcal A)\). Show that \(\sup_n f_n\), \(\inf_n f_n\), \(\limsup_n f_n\), \(\liminf_n f_n\in M(X,\mathcal A)\). Can there exist two functions \(f,g\in M(X,\mathcal A)\) such that \(\max\{f,g\}\notin M(X,\mathcal A)\)? Why?
questions.strategyHtml: The problem wants a sharp yes/no answer, not just a slogan. Decide the claimed implication, then either prove it from the definitions or choose a standard model example, verify the stated convergence/property, and explicitly show the missing conclusion fails.
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ \{ x \in X : h(x) > a \} = \bigcup_{n=1}^\infty \{ x \in X : f_n(x) > a \}. \]
\[ \limsup_{n\to\infty} f_n = \inf_{k\ge 1} \left( \sup_{n \ge k} f_n \right) \quad \text{and} \quad \liminf_{n\to\infty} f_n = \sup_{k\ge 1} \left( \inf_{n \ge k} f_n \right), \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Countable sup and inf reduce to countable unions and intersections of level sets.

Let \((f_n)\) be a sequence of real-valued measurable functions. - Supremum: Let \(h(x) = \sup_{n\ge 1} f_n(x)\). For any \(a \in \mathbb{R}\):

\[ \{ x \in X : h(x) > a \} = \bigcup_{n=1}^\infty \{ x \in X : f_n(x) > a \}. \]
Since each \(\{f_n > a\}\) is measurable, their countable union is measurable. Thus \(\sup_n f_n\) is measurable. - Infimum: \(\inf_n f_n = - \sup_n (-f_n)\), which is measurable. - Limits: The limit superior and limit inferior can be written as:
\[ \limsup_{n\to\infty} f_n = \inf_{k\ge 1} \left( \sup_{n \ge k} f_n \right) \quad \text{and} \quad \liminf_{n\to\infty} f_n = \sup_{k\ge 1} \left( \inf_{n \ge k} f_n \right), \]
which are measurable because they are countable infima/suprema of measurable functions. For any two measurable functions \(f, g\), we have \(\max\{f, g\} = \sup\{f, g\}\). Since this is a finite supremum of measurable functions, it is always measurable. Thus, no such counterexample can exist.
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Prove that \(A\) is measurable if and only if \(\chi_A\) is a measurable function.
questions.strategyHtml: The proof wants measurability reduced to preimages or level sets. Rewrite the target function using countable unions/intersections and known measurable building blocks, then use closure of the sigma-algebra.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: A set is measurable exactly when its characteristic function has measurable level sets.

Let \((X, \mathcal{A})\) be a measurable space and \(A \subset X\). - Forward Direction: Assume \(A \in \mathcal{A}\). The characteristic function is \(\chi_A(x)\). For any \(a \in \mathbb{R}\), the set \(E_a = \{ x \in X : \chi_A(x) > a \}\) is: - \(X \in \mathcal{A}\) if \(a < 0\), - \(A \in \mathcal{A}\) if \(0 \le a < 1\), - \(\varnothing \in \mathcal{A}\) if \(a \ge 1\). In all cases, \(E_a \in \mathcal{A}\). Thus, \(\chi_A\) is a measurable function. - Backward Direction: Assume \(\chi_A\) is a measurable function. Then the set:

\[ A = \{ x \in X : \chi_A(x) > 1/2 \} = \chi_A^{-1}((1/2, \infty)) \]
must belong to \(\mathcal{A}\) by the definition of function measurability. Thus, \(A\) is a measurable set.
questions.finalReason: July exact history

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Prove or disprove: \(f\in M(X,\mathcal A)\Leftrightarrow f^\pm\in M^+(X,\mathcal A)\).
questions.strategyHtml: The problem wants a sharp yes/no answer, not just a slogan. Decide the claimed implication, then either prove it from the definitions or choose a standard model example, verify the stated convergence/property, and explicitly show the missing conclusion fails.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Use f+=max(f,0), f-=max(-f,0); |f| alone forgets the sign.

This equivalence is true. - Proof (Forward direction): Let \(f \in M(X, \mathcal{A})\). The positive and negative parts are \(f^+ = \max\{f, 0\}\) and \(f^- = \max\{-f, 0\}\). Since the functions \(x \mapsto \max\{x, 0\}\) and \(x \mapsto \max\{-x, 0\}\) are continuous, and composition of continuous functions with measurable functions yields measurable functions, both \(f^+\) and \(f^-\) are measurable. Since they are non-negative, \(f^\pm \in M^+(X, \mathcal{A})\). - Proof (Backward direction): Assume \(f^+, f^- \in M^+(X, \mathcal{A})\). Since \(f(x) = f^+(x) - f^-(x)\) for all \(x \in X\), and the difference of two real-valued measurable functions is measurable, we have \(f \in M(X, \mathcal{A})\).
questions.finalReason: July exact history

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Prove or disprove: \(f\in M(X,\mathcal A)\Leftrightarrow |f|\in M(X,\mathcal A)\).
questions.strategyHtml: The problem wants a sharp yes/no answer, not just a slogan. Decide the claimed implication, then either prove it from the definitions or choose a standard model example, verify the stated convergence/property, and explicitly show the missing conclusion fails.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: For measurable-function questions, translate every operation into inverse images of Borel sets.

The forward implication is true, but the backward implication is false. - Forward Implication: If \(f \in M(X, \mathcal{A})\), then \(|f| = g \circ f\), where \(g(t) = |t|\) is a continuous function. The composition of a continuous function with a measurable function is measurable, so \(|f| \in M(X, \mathcal{A})\). - Counterexample for Backward Implication: Let \(A \subset X\) be a non-measurable set (assuming such a set exists, e.g. a Vitali set in \(\mathbb{R}\)). Define:

\[ f(x) = \chi_A(x) - \chi_{X \setminus A}(x). \]
Since \(|f(x)| = 1\) for all \(x \in X\), the function \(|f|\) is constant, and hence measurable. However, \(f^{-1}(\{1\}) = A \notin \mathcal{A}\), so \(f\) is not a measurable function.
questions.finalReason: July exact history

questions.subject: measure
questions.bankRefFull: Theory Questions.pdf: Q1.10, Q1.11, Q1.12, Q1.13
questions.questionHtml: State the Caratheodory measurability condition and the theorem that turns an outer measure into a complete measure.
questions.answerHtml:
Let \(\mu^*\) be an outer measure on \(X\). A set \(E\subset X\) is Caratheodory measurable if for every \(A\subset X\),

\[ \mu^*(A)=\mu^*(A\cap E)+\mu^*(A\setminus E). \]
Since subadditivity always gives \(\le\), it is enough to prove the reverse inequality. Every set of outer measure zero is Caratheodory measurable. The Caratheodory theorem states that the family of all \(\mu^*\)-measurable sets is a \(\sigma\)-algebra and the restriction of \(\mu^*\) to it is a complete measure.
questions.finalReason: repeated core ID

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Explain the construction of Lebesgue measure on \(\mathbb R\) and on \(\mathbb R^N\).
questions.answerHtml:
Start from the Lebesgue outer measure. In \(\mathbb R\), cover \(E\subset\mathbb R\) by countable families of intervals and set

\[ \lambda^*(E)=\inf\left\{\sum_{k=1}^\infty |I_k|: E\subset\bigcup_{k=1}^\infty I_k\right\}. \]
In \(\mathbb R^N\), use rectangles or boxes and replace \(|I_k|\) by their \(N\)-dimensional volume. Lebesgue measurable sets are the Caratheodory measurable sets for this outer measure, and Lebesgue measure is the restriction of \(\lambda^*\) to that \(\sigma\)-algebra.
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Write the definition of integrable functions, the Lebesgue integral, and \(L^1\).
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ \int_X f\,d\mu=\int_X f^+\,d\mu-\int_X f^-\,d\mu. \]
\[ L^1(X,\mu)=\{f:\int_X |f|\,d\mu<\infty\}/\sim. \]
questions.answerHtml:
For a measurable real-valued function \(f\), write \(f=f^+-f^-\), where \(f^+=\max\{f,0\}\) and \(f^-=\max\{-f,0\}\). The function \(f\) is integrable if both \(\int f^+\,d\mu\) and \(\int f^-\,d\mu\) are finite. Then

\[ \int_X f\,d\mu=\int_X f^+\,d\mu-\int_X f^-\,d\mu. \]
The space \(L^1(X,\mu)\) is the vector space of measurable functions with finite integral of the absolute value, modulo equality a.e.:
\[ L^1(X,\mu)=\{f:\int_X |f|\,d\mu<\infty\}/\sim. \]

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Write the definitions of nonnegative simple function, its canonical form, and the Lebesgue integral of a nonnegative simple function.
questions.strategyHtml: This is a definition question, but the trap is the canonical form. Use disjoint measurable level sets and remember the convention \(0\cdot\infty=0\).
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ s=\sum_{j=1}^m a_j\chi_{A_j}, \]
\[ \int_X s\,d\mu=\sum_{j=1}^m a_j\mu(A_j), \]
questions.answerHtml:
A nonnegative simple function is a measurable function taking only finitely many nonnegative values. If the distinct nonzero values are \(a_1,\dots,a_m\), its canonical form is

\[ s=\sum_{j=1}^m a_j\chi_{A_j}, \]
where \(A_j=\{x:s(x)=a_j\}\in\mathcal A\) and the \(A_j\)'s are pairwise disjoint. If the value \(0\) is included, its term contributes nothing. The Lebesgue integral of \(s\) is
\[ \int_X s\,d\mu=\sum_{j=1}^m a_j\mu(A_j), \]
with the convention \(0\cdot\infty=0\).

The definition is independent of non-canonical representations because any two finite measurable partitions can be refined by intersections; on the common refinement the sums agree term by term.
questions.finalReason: exact Jan/Feb-2026 ID; not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Write the two equivalent definitions of the Lebesgue integral for a nonnegative measurable function.
questions.strategyHtml: State both versions and explain why they agree. The bridge is the Simple Approximation Theorem plus Monotone Convergence.
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ \int_X f\,d\mu=\sup\left\{\int_X s\,d\mu:\ 0\le s\le f,\ s\text{ simple}\right\}. \]
\[ \int_X f\,d\mu=\lim_{n\to\infty}\int_X s_n\,d\mu. \]
questions.answerHtml:
For \(f\in M^+(X,\mathcal A)\), the first definition is approximation from below by simple functions:

\[ \int_X f\,d\mu=\sup\left\{\int_X s\,d\mu:\ 0\le s\le f,\ s\text{ simple}\right\}. \]
Equivalently, choose any increasing sequence of nonnegative simple functions \(s_n\uparrow f\) pointwise a.e. and define
\[ \int_X f\,d\mu=\lim_{n\to\infty}\int_X s_n\,d\mu. \]
The Simple Approximation Theorem guarantees such a sequence exists. The first definition gives \(\int s_n\le\sup\{\int s:0\le s\le f\}\), so the limit is at most the supremum. Conversely, if \(0\le s\le f\) is simple, then \(s_n\uparrow f\) implies \(\int s\le\lim_n\int s_n\) by the same approximation argument used in the proof of Monotone Convergence. Taking the supremum over \(s\) gives equality.
questions.finalReason: exact Jan/Feb-2026 ID; not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: State Chebyshev's inequality and the absolute-continuity property of the Lebesgue integral.
questions.strategyHtml: Convert a level-set estimate into an integral estimate. For absolute continuity, split \(f\) into a bounded part and a small tail.
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ \mu(\{f\ge a\})\le \frac1a\int_X f\,d\mu. \]
\[ \int_X f\,d\mu\ge\int_X a\chi_{E_a}\,d\mu=a\mu(E_a). \]
questions.answerHtml:
If \(f\in M^+(X,\mathcal A)\) and \(a>0\), Chebyshev's inequality is

\[ \mu(\{f\ge a\})\le \frac1a\int_X f\,d\mu. \]
Proof: On \(E_a=\{f\ge a\}\), one has \(f\ge a\chi_{E_a}\). By monotonicity of the integral,
\[ \int_X f\,d\mu\ge\int_X a\chi_{E_a}\,d\mu=a\mu(E_a). \]

If \(f\in L^1(X)\), then \(E\mapsto\int_E |f|\,d\mu\) is absolutely continuous with respect to \(\mu\): for every \(\varepsilon>0\), there is \(\delta>0\) such that \(\mu(E)<\delta\) implies \(\int_E |f|\,d\mu<\varepsilon\). To prove it, choose \(M\) so large that \(\int_{\{|f|>M\}}|f|\,d\mu<\varepsilon/2\). Then

\[ \int_E |f|\,d\mu\le M\mu(E)+\int_{\{|f|>M\}}|f|\,d\mu. \]
Taking \(\delta=\varepsilon/(2M)\) gives the result.
questions.finalReason: not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: measure
questions.chapter: 8. Classical Examples, Product Measure, And Radon-Nikodym
questions.questionHtml: Define the Cantor set and the Vitali-Lebesgue function, and state their key properties.
questions.strategyHtml: These are example questions. Memorize construction, properties, and the conceptual warning each object provides.
questions.answerHtml:
The Cantor set \(C\) is obtained from \([0,1]\) by removing the open middle third, then the open middle third of each remaining interval, and so on. Equivalently,

\[ C=\bigcap_{n=0}^{\infty} C_n, \]
where \(C_n\) is the union of \(2^n\) closed intervals of length \(3^{-n}\).

Its key properties are: \(C\) is closed and compact; it is perfect, meaning it has no isolated points; it is nowhere dense; it is uncountable; and \(\lambda(C)=0\), since the total length removed is \(\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}2^{n-1}3^{-n}=1\).

The Vitali-Lebesgue function, also called the Cantor function, is the continuous nondecreasing function \(F:[0,1]\to[0,1]\) that is constant on every interval removed in the construction of \(C\), and maps \(C\) onto \([0,1]\). It is the standard warning example: \(F\) is continuous and monotone, \(F'=0\) a.e., but \(F(1)-F(0)=1\), so it is not absolutely continuous.
questions.finalReason: not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: measure
questions.chapter: 8. Classical Examples, Product Measure, And Radon-Nikodym
questions.questionHtml: Write the definitions of derivative of a measure and absolutely continuous measure. State the Radon-Nikodym theorem.
questions.answerHtml:
A measure \( u\) is absolutely continuous with respect to \(\mu\), written \( u\ll\mu\), if \(\mu(E)=0\) implies \( u(E)=0\). A density, or derivative, of \( u\) with respect to \(\mu\) is a measurable function \(f\ge0\) such that

\[ u(E)=\int_E f\,d\mu\qquad orall E\in\mathcal A. \]
The Radon-Nikodym theorem says that if \( u\ll\mu\) and \(\mu, u\) are \(\sigma\)-finite measures, then there exists a measurable \(f\ge0\), unique up to equality \(\mu\)-a.e., such that \( u(E)=\int_E f\,d\mu\) for every measurable \(E\). We write \(f= rac{d u}{d\mu}\).
questions.finalReason: not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: measure
questions.chapter: 8. Classical Examples, Product Measure, And Radon-Nikodym
questions.questionHtml: State the product \(\sigma\)-algebra/product measure construction and its relation with Lebesgue measure on product spaces.
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ \mathcal A\otimes\mathcal B=\sigma(\{A\times B:\ A\in\mathcal A,\ B\in\mathcal B\}). \]
\[ (\mu\otimes\nu)(A\times B)=\mu(A)\nu(B). \]
questions.answerHtml:
Given measurable spaces \((X,\mathcal A)\), \((Y,\mathcal B)\), the product \(\sigma\)-algebra is

\[ \mathcal A\otimes\mathcal B=\sigma(\{A\times B:\ A\in\mathcal A,\ B\in\mathcal B\}). \]
If \(\mu,\nu\) are \(\sigma\)-finite measures, there is a unique product measure \(\mu\otimes\nu\) on \(\mathcal A\otimes\mathcal B\) satisfying
\[ (\mu\otimes\nu)(A\times B)=\mu(A)\nu(B). \]
For Euclidean spaces, Lebesgue measure on \(\mathbb R^{m+n}\) agrees with the product of Lebesgue measures on \(\mathbb R^m\) and \(\mathbb R^n\) on the corresponding product \(\sigma\)-algebra.

questions.subject: measure
questions.chapter: 9. Measure Structure, Regularity, And Vanishing Lemma
questions.questionHtml: Given a metric space \((X,d)\), write the definition of its Borel \(\sigma\)-algebra \(\mathcal B(X)\). Provide classes of Borel sets and characterize \(\mathcal B(\mathbb R)\), \(\mathcal B(\mathbb R^N)\).
questions.keyStepsHtml: \[ \mathcal{B}(X) = \sigma(\{ U \subset X : U \text{ is open} \}). \]
questions.answerHtml:
For a metric space \((X, d)\), the Borel \(\sigma\)-algebra \(\mathcal{B}(X)\) is defined as the smallest \(\sigma\)-algebra containing all open subsets of \(X\):

\[ \mathcal{B}(X) = \sigma(\{ U \subset X : U \text{ is open} \}). \]
Classes of Borel sets include: 1. Open Sets and Closed Sets. 2. \(F_\sigma\) sets: Countable unions of closed sets. 3. \(G_\delta\) sets: Countable intersections of open sets. 4. \(F_{\sigma\delta}\) sets (countable intersections of \(F_\sigma\) sets) and \(G_{\delta\sigma}\) sets (countable unions of \(G_\delta\) sets). Characterization on \(\mathbb{R}\): \(\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})\) can be generated by any of the following families of intervals (with endpoints in \(\mathbb{R}\) or \(\mathbb{Q}\)): - Open intervals: \(\{(a, b) : a, b \in \mathbb{R}\}\). - Closed intervals: \(\{[a, b] : a, b \in \mathbb{R}\}\). - Half-open intervals: \(\{(a, b] : a, b \in \mathbb{R}\}\) or \(\{[a, b) : a, b \in \mathbb{R}\}\). - Unbounded open intervals: \(\{(-\infty, a) : a \in \mathbb{R}\}\) or \(\{(a, \infty) : a \in \mathbb{R}\}\). Characterization on \(\mathbb{R}^N\): \(\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R}^N)\) is generated by open balls, by open rectangles of the form \(\prod_{j=1}^N (a_j, b_j)\), or by rational rectangles.
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: measure
questions.chapter: 9. Measure Structure, Regularity, And Vanishing Lemma
questions.questionHtml: Prove the continuity of the measure along monotone sequences, either increasing or decreasing, under suitable further assumptions.
questions.strategyHtml: The task asks for the two monotone-set limit rules. For increasing sets, decompose into disjoint increments; for decreasing sets, reduce to the increasing case by complements and use the finite-measure hypothesis.
questions.answerHtml:
Let \((X, \mathcal{A}, \mu)\) be a measure space. 1. Increasing Sequences: If \(E_n \uparrow E = \bigcup_{n=1}^\infty E_n\), then \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \mu(E_n) = \mu(E)\). - Proof: Define \(F_1 = E_1\), and \(F_n = E_n \setminus E_{n-1}\) for all \(n \ge 2\). The sets \((F_n)\) are pairwise disjoint and satisfy \(\bigcup_{n=1}^N F_n = E_N\) and \(\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty F_n = E\). By countable additivity:

\[ \mu(E) = \sum_{n=1}^\infty \mu(F_n) = \lim_{N\to\infty} \sum_{n=1}^N \mu(F_n) = \lim_{N\to\infty} \mu(E_N). \]
2. Decreasing Sequences: If \(E_n \downarrow E = \bigcap_{n=1}^\infty E_n\) and \(\mu(E_1) < \infty\), then \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \mu(E_n) = \mu(E)\). - Proof: Define \(A_n = E_1 \setminus E_n\). Since \(E_n\) is decreasing, \(A_n\) is increasing: \(A_n \uparrow \bigcup (E_1 \setminus E_n) = E_1 \setminus E\). Applying the increasing case yields:
\[ \lim_{n\to\infty} \mu(E_1 \setminus E_n) = \mu(E_1 \setminus E). \]
Since \(\mu(E_1) < \infty\), we can write \(\lim_{n\to\infty} (\mu(E_1) - \mu(E_n)) = \mu(E_1) - \mu(E) \implies \lim_{n\to\infty} \mu(E_n) = \mu(E)\). - Counterexample for decreasing sequences without finite measure: Let \(X = \mathbb{R}\) with Lebesgue measure \(\lambda\). Let \(E_n = [n, \infty)\). We have \(E_n \downarrow \varnothing\), so \(\lambda(\bigcap E_n) = 0\). However, \(\lambda(E_n) = \infty\) for every \(n \ge 1\). Thus, \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \lambda(E_n) = \infty \neq 0\).
questions.finalReason: repeated core ID

questions.subject: measure
questions.chapter: 9. Measure Structure, Regularity, And Vanishing Lemma
questions.questionHtml: State and prove the theorem about regularity of the Lebesgue measure.
questions.strategyHtml: The proof is an approximation argument. State inner and outer regularity, prove it first for convenient measurable sets, then extend by monotone approximation and the definition of Lebesgue measure.
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ \lambda(E) = \inf \{ \lambda(O) : E \subset O, \, O \text{ open} \} \quad (\text{Outer Regularity}) \]
\[ \lambda(E) = \sup \{ \lambda(K) : K \subset E, \, K \text{ compact} \} \quad (\text{Inner Regularity}) \]
questions.answerHtml:
Lebesgue measure \(\lambda\) on \(\mathbb{R}^d\) is regular: for any Lebesgue measurable set \(E \subset \mathbb{R}^d\):

\[ \lambda(E) = \inf \{ \lambda(O) : E \subset O, \, O \text{ open} \} \quad (\text{Outer Regularity}) \]
\[ \lambda(E) = \sup \{ \lambda(K) : K \subset E, \, K \text{ compact} \} \quad (\text{Inner Regularity}) \]
Proof: - Outer Regularity: By definition of the Lebesgue outer measure, \(\lambda(E) = \inf \sum_{j=1}^\infty |I_j|\), where the infimum is taken over all countable coverings of \(E\) by open boxes \(I_j\). For any \(\varepsilon > 0\), choose a covering of \(E\) by open boxes \(I_j\) such that \(\sum_{j=1}^\infty |I_j| < \lambda(E) + \varepsilon\). Let \(O = \bigcup_{j=1}^\infty I_j\). Then \(O\) is an open set containing \(E\), and by subadditivity:
\[ \lambda(O) \le \sum_{j=1}^\infty \lambda(I_j) = \sum_{j=1}^\infty |I_j| < \lambda(E) + \varepsilon. \]
Taking the infimum over all open sets \(O \supset E\) yields Outer Regularity. - Inner Regularity: - Case 1: \(E\) is bounded. Let \(E\) be contained in a bounded open box \(U \subset \mathbb{R}^d\). Its complement in \(U\), \(F = U \setminus E\), is also measurable. By Outer Regularity applied to \(F\), for any \(\varepsilon > 0\), there exists an open set \(O \supset F\) such that \(\lambda(O) < \lambda(F) + \varepsilon\). Let \(K = U \setminus O\). Since \(O\) is open, \(K\) is closed, and since \(K \subset U\), \(K\) is bounded, so \(K\) is compact. Since \(O \supset U \setminus E\), we have \(K = U \setminus O \subset E\). We have \(\lambda(U) = \lambda(O) + \lambda(K)\) and \(\lambda(U) = \lambda(E) + \lambda(F)\). Therefore:
\[ \lambda(E) = \lambda(U) - \lambda(F) > \lambda(U) - (\lambda(O) - \varepsilon) = \lambda(K) + \varepsilon. \]
Thus \(\lambda(K) > \lambda(E) - \varepsilon\), proving Inner Regularity for bounded sets. - Case 2: \(E\) is unbounded. For each \(R \ge 1\), define \(E_R = E \cap B_R(0)\). Since \(E_R \uparrow E\), continuity of measure from below implies \(\lambda(E_R) \to \lambda(E)\). For any \(L < \lambda(E)\), choose \(R\) large enough such that \(\lambda(E_R) > L\). Since \(E_R\) is bounded, Case 1 yields a compact set \(K \subset E_R \subset E\) such that \(\lambda(K) > L\), proving Inner Regularity generally.
questions.finalReason: exact Jan/Feb-2026 ID; repeated core ID

questions.subject: measure
questions.chapter: 9. Measure Structure, Regularity, And Vanishing Lemma
questions.questionHtml: State and prove the Vanishing Lemma in the following two cases: for nonnegative measurable functions and for functions belonging to \(L^1\).
questions.strategyHtml: The proof wants measurability reduced to preimages or level sets. Rewrite the target function using countable unions/intersections and known measurable building blocks, then use closure of the sigma-algebra.
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ 0 = \int_X f \, d\mu \ge \int_{E_n} f \, d\mu \ge \frac{1}{n} \mu(E_n) \implies \mu(E_n) = 0. \]
\[ \mu(\{ f > 0 \}) \le \sum_{n=1}^\infty \mu(E_n) = 0. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Case 1 (Nonnegative functions): If \(f \in M^+(X, \mathcal{A})\) and \(\int_X f \, d\mu = 0\), then \(f = 0\) a.e.

Proof: For each \(n \ge 1\), define the set \(E_n = \{ x \in X : f(x) \ge 1/n \}\). By Chebyshev's inequality:

\[ 0 = \int_X f \, d\mu \ge \int_{E_n} f \, d\mu \ge \frac{1}{n} \mu(E_n) \implies \mu(E_n) = 0. \]
The set where \(f\) is positive is \(\{ x \in X : f(x) > 0 \} = \bigcup_{n=1}^\infty E_n\). By countable subadditivity:
\[ \mu(\{ f > 0 \}) \le \sum_{n=1}^\infty \mu(E_n) = 0. \]
Thus \(f = 0\) a.e.

Case 2 (L^1 functions): If \(f \in L^1(X, \mu)\) and \(\int_E f \, d\mu = 0\) for every measurable set \(E \in \mathcal{A}\), then \(f = 0\) a.e.

Proof: Let \(E_+ = \{ x \in X : f(x) > 0 \}\) and \(E_- = \{ x \in X : f(x) < 0 \}\). Both sets are measurable. By hypothesis:

\[ \int_{E_+} f \, d\mu = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \int_{E_-} f \, d\mu = 0. \]
Since \(f = f^+\) on \(E_+\) and \(f = -f^-\) on \(E_-\), we have:
\[ \int_X f^+ \, d\mu = \int_{E_+} f \, d\mu = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \int_X f^- \, d\mu = \int_{E_-} (-f) \, d\mu = 0. \]
Applying Case 1 to the nonnegative functions \(f^+\) and \(f^-\) yields \(f^+ = 0\) a.e. and \(f^- = 0\) a.e. Thus \(f = f^+ - f^- = 0\) a.e.
questions.finalReason: exact Jun-2026 ID; July exact history

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: State the Hahn-Banach theorem in the continuous extension form.
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ F(y) = f(y) \qquad \forall y \in Y \quad (\text{i.e., } F|_Y = f), \]
\[ \|F\|_{X^*} = \|f\|_{Y^*}. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Hahn-Banach extension preserves the norm: extend f from Y to X without paying anything.

Let \(X\) be a normed space and let \(Y\subset X\) be a vector subspace. If \(f\in Y^*\), then there exists \(F\in X^*\) such that

\[ F|_Y=f,\qquad \|F\|_{X^*}=\|f\|_{Y^*}. \]

Thus every continuous linear functional on a subspace has a continuous linear extension to the whole space with exactly the same norm.
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Let \(Y\) be a vector subspace of \(X=\mathbb R^2\). Given \(f\in Y^*\), is it possible to find \(F\in X^*\) such that \(F=f\) in \(Y\) and \(\|F\|_{X^*}=\|f\|_{Y^*}\)?
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Hahn-Banach is extension without increasing the norm; most corollaries start on span{x0}.

Yes. Since \(X = \mathbb{R}^2\) is a finite-dimensional normed space and \(Y \subset \mathbb{R}^2\) is a linear subspace, the continuous extension form of the Hahn-Banach Theorem applies directly. Any continuous linear functional \(f\) on \(Y\) has a continuous linear extension \(F \in X^*\) satisfying:

\[ F|_Y = f \quad \text{and} \quad \|F\|_{X^*} = \|f\|_{Y^*}. \]
Since every linear functional on a finite-dimensional space is continuous, the continuity conditions are automatically satisfied for any norm on \(\mathbb{R}^2\).
questions.finalReason: not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: State and prove the three corollaries of the Hahn-Banach Theorem.
questions.strategyHtml: Each corollary is Hahn-Banach applied to the right one-dimensional or quotient-like subspace. Define the small functional first, compute its norm exactly, extend it, then read off separation or norm-attainment.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: The corollaries come from extending the one-dimensional functional that norms x0.

Corollary 1: For any \(x_0 \in X\) with \(x_0 \neq 0\), there exists \(L \in X^*\) such that \(\|L\|_{X^*} = 1\) and \(L(x_0) = \|x_0\|_X\).

Proof: Let \(Y = \operatorname{span}\{x_0\} = \{\alpha x_0 : \alpha \in \mathbb{K}\}\). Define the linear functional \(l : Y \to \mathbb{K}\) by \(l(\alpha x_0) = \alpha \|x_0\|_X\). We compute the norm of \(l\): for any \(y = \alpha x_0 \in Y\), we have \(|l(y)| = |\alpha| \|x_0\|_X = \|\alpha x_0\|_X = \|y\|_X\). Thus, \(l\) is continuous and bounded with \(\|l\|_{Y^*} = 1\). By the continuous form of the Hahn-Banach Theorem, there exists a continuous linear functional \(L \in X^*\) extending \(l\) to \(X\) such that \(L|_Y = l\) and \(\|L\|_{X^*} = \||l\|_{Y^*} = 1\). In particular, \(L(x_0) = l(x_0) = \|x_0\|_X\).

Corollary 2: The dual space \(X^*\) separates points: if \(x, y \in X\) with \(x \neq y\), there exists \(L \in X^*\) such that \(L(x) \neq L(y)\).

Proof: Since \(x \neq y\), we have \(x - y \neq 0\). Applying Corollary 1 to \(x_0 = x - y\), there exists \(L \in X^*\) with \(\|L\|_{X^*} = 1\) and \(L(x - y) = \|x - y\|_X \neq 0\). By linearity of \(L\), \(L(x) - L(y) \neq 0\), so \(L(x) \neq L(y)\).

Corollary 3: Let \(Y\) be a linear subspace of a normed space \(X\). If \(x_0 \in X \setminus \overline{Y}\), then there exists \(L \in X^*\) such that \(L|_Y = 0\), \(L(x_0) = \operatorname{dist}(x_0, Y)\), and \(\|L\|_{X^*} = 1\).

Proof: Let \(d = \operatorname{dist}(x_0, Y) = \inf_{y \in Y} \|x_0 - y\|_X\). Since \(\overline{Y}\) is closed and \(x_0 \notin \overline{Y}\), we have \(d > 0\). Define the subspace \(Z = Y + \operatorname{span}\{x_0\} = \{y + \alpha x_0 : y \in Y, \alpha \in \mathbb{K}\}\). Since \(x_0 \notin Y\), each element in \(Z\) has a unique representation. Define the linear functional \(l : Z \to \mathbb{K}\) by \(l(y + \alpha x_0) = \alpha d\). Clearly, \(l|_Y = 0\) and \(l(x_0) = d\). We show \(\|l\|_{Z^*} = 1\). For any \(z = y + \alpha x_0 \in Z\): - If \(\alpha = 0\), \(l(z) = 0 \le \|z\|_X\). - If \(\alpha \neq 0\), \(|l(z)| = |\alpha| d = |\alpha| \inf_{y' \in Y} \|x_0 - y'\|_X \le |\alpha| \|x_0 - (-y/\alpha)\|_X = \|y + \alpha x_0\|_X = \|z\|_X\). This proves \(|l(z)| \le \|z\|_X\) for all \(z \in Z\), hence \(\|l\|_{Z^*} \le 1\). By definition of the infimum, for any \(\varepsilon > 0\) there exists \(y_\varepsilon \in Y\) such that \(\|x_0 - y_\varepsilon\|_X < d + \varepsilon\). Letting \(z_\varepsilon = x_0 - y_\varepsilon \in Z\), we have \(l(z_\varepsilon) = d\). Thus \(\|l\|_{Z^*} \ge \frac{|l(z_\varepsilon)|}{\|z_\varepsilon\|_X} > \frac{d}{d + \varepsilon}\). Taking \(\varepsilon \downarrow 0\) yields \(\|l\|_{Z^*} \ge 1\). Hence \(\|l\|_{Z^*} = 1\). By the Hahn-Banach Theorem, there exists \(L \in X^*\) extending \(l\) such that \(L|_Z = l\) and \(\|L\|_{X^*} = \|l\|_{Z^*} = 1\). Thus \(L|_Y = 0\) and \(L(x_0) = d = \operatorname{dist}(x_0, Y)\).
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Let \(X\) be a normed space, let \(H\) be a hyperplane of \(X\). When do we say that \(H\) separates or strictly separates \(A\) and \(B\)?
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ H = \{x \in X : L(x) = \alpha\}, \]
\[ L(a) \le \alpha \le L(b) \qquad \forall a \in A, \, \forall b \in B. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Hahn-Banach separation is a norm-preserving functional turned into a hyperplane inequality.

Let \(X\) be a real normed space (or the real part of functionals if \(X\) is complex). A hyperplane \(H\) is a set of the form:

\[ H = \{x \in X : L(x) = \alpha\}, \]
where \(L \in X^*\) is a continuous linear functional with \(L \neq 0\) and \(\alpha \in \mathbb{R}\). - \(H\) separates two subsets \(A, B \subset X\) if:
\[ L(a) \le \alpha \le L(b) \qquad \forall a \in A, \, \forall b \in B. \]
- \(H\) strictly separates \(A\) and \(B\) if there exists \(\varepsilon > 0\) such that:
\[ L(a) \le \alpha - \varepsilon \quad \text{and} \quad L(b) \ge \alpha + \varepsilon \qquad \forall a \in A, \, \forall b \in B. \]
