questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: State the Hahn-Banach theorem in the separation form, first version.
questions.keyStepsHtml: \[ L(a) < \alpha \le L(b) \qquad \forall a \in A, \, \forall b \in B. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: First separation theorem: disjoint convex sets with one open can be separated by a closed hyperplane.

Let \(X\) be a normed space. Let \(A,B\subset X\) be nonempty, disjoint, convex sets, and assume that \(A\) is open. Then there exist \(f\in X^*\), \(f\ne0\), and \(\alpha\in\mathbb R\) such that

\[ f(a)\le\alpha\le f(b)\qquad\forall a\in A,\ \forall b\in B. \]

The closed hyperplane \(H=\{x\in X:f(x)=\alpha\}\) separates \(A\) and \(B\). The openness of \(A\) is the hypothesis that gives a continuous separating functional.
questions.finalReason: not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: State the Hahn-Banach theorem in the separation form, second version.
questions.keyStepsHtml: \[ L(a) \le \alpha < \beta \le L(b) \qquad \forall a \in A, \, \forall b \in B. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Strict separation comes from positive distance: fatten the closed set, separate, then keep a gap.

Let \(X\) be a normed space. Let \(A,B\subset X\) be nonempty, disjoint, convex sets. If \(A\) is closed and \(B\) is compact, then there exist \(f\in X^*\), \(f\ne0\), \(\alpha\in\mathbb R\), and \(\varepsilon>0\) such that

\[ f(a)\le\alpha-\varepsilon,\qquad f(b)\ge\alpha+\varepsilon \]

for every \(a\in A\), \(b\in B\). Thus the hyperplane \(H=\{f=\alpha\}\) strictly separates \(A\) and \(B\).

A standard proof is to use that \(\operatorname{dist}(A,B)>0\) when one set is closed, the other compact, and they are disjoint. Enlarge \(A\) slightly by an open ball of radius smaller than this distance; the enlarged set is open and convex and remains disjoint from \(B\). Apply the first separation theorem, then the enlargement gives a positive gap.
questions.finalReason: not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 2. Weak Convergence, Weak-Star Convergence, And Banach-Alaoglu
questions.questionHtml: Write the definition of weak convergence.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: For weak questions, never test vectors directly; test them through every continuous linear functional.

A sequence \((x_n)\) in a normed space \(X\) converges weakly to \(x \in X\), denoted by \(x_n \rightharpoonup x\), if:

\[ \lim_{n\to\infty} L(x_n) = L(x) \qquad \forall L \in X^*. \]
This means the sequence of scalars \((L(x_n))\) converges to the scalar \(L(x)\) for every continuous linear functional on \(X\).
questions.finalReason: repeated core ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 2. Weak Convergence, Weak-Star Convergence, And Banach-Alaoglu
questions.questionHtml: How can weak convergence be formulated in \(L^p\) and in \(\ell^p\)?
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ \lim_{n\to\infty} \int_X f_n g \, d\mu = \int_X fg \, d\mu \qquad \forall g \in L^q(X, \mu). \]
\[ \lim_{n\to\infty} \int_X f_n g \, d\mu = \int_X fg \, d\mu \qquad \forall g \in L^\infty(X, \mu). \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: For weak questions, never test vectors directly; test them through every continuous linear functional.

In \(L^p(X, \mu)\) spaces: - For \(1 < p < \infty\), by the Riesz Representation Theorem, the dual of \(L^p(X, \mu)\) is \(L^q(X, \mu)\) where \(\frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1\). Thus, \(f_n \rightharpoonup f\) weakly in \(L^p(X, \mu)\) if:

\[ \lim_{n\to\infty} \int_X f_n g \, d\mu = \int_X fg \, d\mu \qquad \forall g \in L^q(X, \mu). \]
- For \(p = 1\), on a \(\sigma\)-finite measure space, the dual is \(L^\infty(X, \mu)\). Thus, \(f_n \rightharpoonup f\) weakly in \(L^1(X, \mu)\) if:
\[ \lim_{n\to\infty} \int_X f_n g \, d\mu = \int_X fg \, d\mu \qquad \forall g \in L^\infty(X, \mu). \]

In \(\ell^p\) sequence spaces: - For \(1 \le p < \infty\), weak convergence \(x_n \rightharpoonup x\) means:

\[ \lim_{n\to\infty} \sum_{k=1}^\infty x_n(k) y(k) = \sum_{k=1}^\infty x(k) y(k) \qquad \forall y \in \ell^q, \]
where \(\frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1\) (with \(q = \infty\) when \(p=1\)). A sequence \((x_n)\) converges weakly to \(x\) in \(\ell^p\) if and only if it is norm-bounded and converges coordinatewise, i.e., \(\lim_{n\to\infty} x_n(k) = x(k)\) for each \(k \ge 1\).
questions.finalReason: repeated core ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 2. Weak Convergence, Weak-Star Convergence, And Banach-Alaoglu
questions.questionHtml: Show that strong convergence implies weak convergence.
questions.strategyHtml: Work from the definition: weak convergence means testing against every continuous functional. So fix an arbitrary functional, estimate its value on the norm-convergent difference, and let the operator norm carry the limit to zero.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Strong implies weak by the inequality |L(xn-x)| <= ||L|| ||xn-x||.

Let \((x_n)\) be a sequence in a normed space \(X\) that converges strongly (in norm) to \(x \in X\), meaning \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \|x_n - x\|_X = 0\). Let \(L \in X^*\) be any continuous linear functional. By linearity and boundedness of \(L\):

\[ |L(x_n) - L(x)| = |L(x_n - x)| \le \|L\|_{X^*} \|x_n - x\|_X. \]
Since \(\|L\|_{X^*}\) is finite and \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \|x_n - x\|_X = 0\), we have \(\lim_{n\to\infty} |L(x_n) - L(x)| = 0\). This holds for all \(L \in X^*\), which proves that \(x_n \rightharpoonup x\) weakly.
questions.finalReason: repeated core ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 2. Weak Convergence, Weak-Star Convergence, And Banach-Alaoglu
questions.questionHtml: Provide a counterexample for the converse implication.
questions.strategyHtml: The problem wants a sharp yes/no answer, not just a slogan. Decide the claimed implication, then either prove it from the definitions or choose a standard model example, verify the stated convergence/property, and explicitly show the missing conclusion fails.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Use the canonical basis: weakly goes to zero in Hilbert/reflexive settings, norm stays one.

Consider the Hilbert space \(H = \ell^2\) and the sequence \((e_n)_{n=1}^\infty\) of standard orthonormal basis vectors, where \(e_n(k) = \delta_{nk}\). By the Riesz Representation Theorem, any continuous linear functional \(L \in (\ell^2)^*\) is of the form \(L(x) = \langle x, y \rangle\) for some \(y \in \ell^2\). For this functional, we have:

\[ L(e_n) = \langle e_n, y \rangle = \overline{y(n)}. \]
Since \(y \in \ell^2\), we have \(\sum_{k=1}^\infty |y(k)|^2 < \infty\), which implies \(\lim_{n\to\infty} y(n) = 0\). Thus, \(\lim_{n\to\infty} L(e_n) = 0 = L(0)\) for all \(L \in (\ell^2)^*\), proving \(e_n \rightharpoonup 0\) weakly. However, for all \(n \ge 1\), we have \(\|e_n - 0\|_2 = \|e_n\|_2 = 1\). Since the norm of the difference does not converge to \(0\), the sequence \((e_n)\) does not converge strongly to \(0\).
questions.finalReason: repeated core ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 2. Weak Convergence, Weak-Star Convergence, And Banach-Alaoglu
questions.questionHtml: Show that the weak limit is unique.
questions.strategyHtml: To prove uniqueness, assume two weak limits and separate them. Hahn-Banach gives a functional detecting their difference, while weak convergence forces the two tested scalar limits to coincide.
questions.keyStepsHtml: \[ L(x) = \lim_{n\to\infty} L(x_n) = L(y) \implies L(x - y) = 0. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Weak limits are unique because continuous linear functionals separate points.

Let \((x_n)\) be a sequence in a normed space \(X\). Suppose \(x_n \rightharpoonup x\) and \(x_n \rightharpoonup y\). Then for any continuous linear functional \(L \in X^*\), we have:

\[ L(x) = \lim_{n\to\infty} L(x_n) = L(y) \implies L(x - y) = 0. \]
Suppose for contradiction that \(x - y \neq 0\). By a corollary of the Hahn-Banach Theorem, there exists \(L_0 \in X^*\) such that \(L_0(x - y) = \|x - y\|_X \neq 0\). This contradicts the fact that \(L(x-y) = 0\) for all \(L \in X^*\). Hence, we must have \(x - y = 0 \implies x = y\), proving that the weak limit is unique.
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 2. Weak Convergence, Weak-Star Convergence, And Banach-Alaoglu
questions.questionHtml: If \(\{x_n\}\) weakly converges to \(x\), can \(\{x_n\}\) be unbounded?
questions.strategyHtml: The target is boundedness from pointwise bounded scalar tests. Translate the sequence into evaluation maps on the dual, apply Uniform Boundedness, then return through the canonical embedding to bound the original norms.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Weakly convergent sequences are bounded, and norm is weakly lower semicontinuous.

No, a weakly convergent sequence is always bounded. Suppose \(x_n \rightharpoonup x\) in a normed space \(X\). Then for every \(L \in X^*\), the sequence of scalars \((L(x_n))\) is convergent, and hence bounded:

\[ \sup_{n \ge 1} |L(x_n)| < \infty \qquad \forall L \in X^*. \]
Let \(\tau : X \to X^{**}\) be the canonical evaluation map, where \(\tau(x_n)(L) = L(x_n)\). The dual space \(X^*\) is a Banach space. The family of linear operators \(\{\tau(x_n)\}_{n=1}^\infty \subset L(X^*, \mathbb{K})\) is pointwise bounded on \(X^*\). By the Uniform Boundedness Principle, this family is bounded in operator norm:
\[ \sup_{n \ge 1} \|\tau(x_n)\|_{X^{**}} < \infty. \]
Since \(\tau\) is an isometric embedding, \(\|\tau(x_n)\|_{X^{**}} = \|x_n\|_X\) for all \(n \ge 1\). Thus, \(\sup_{n \ge 1} \|x_n\|_X < \infty\).
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 2. Weak Convergence, Weak-Star Convergence, And Banach-Alaoglu
questions.questionHtml: State and prove lower semicontinuity with respect to weak convergence of \(x\mapsto\|x\|\).
questions.strategyHtml: The conclusion is a norm inequality under weak convergence. Test the weak limit with unit dual functionals, pass to the limit in scalars, bound by the liminf of the norms, then take the supremum using Hahn-Banach.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Separate the weak limit by Hahn-Banach: choose \(L\) with \(\|L\|=1\) and \(L(x)=\|x\|\).

Theorem. Let \(X\) be a normed space and let \(x_n\rightharpoonup x\) weakly in \(X\). Then the norm is weakly lower semicontinuous:

\[ \|x\|_X\le \liminf_{n\to\infty}\|x_n\|_X. \]

Proof. If \(x=0\), the result is immediate. Assume \(x\ne0\). By the Hahn-Banach corollary, there exists \(L\in X^*\) such that

\[ \|L\|_{X^*}=1, \qquad L(x)=\|x\|_X. \]

Since \(x_n\rightharpoonup x\), we have \(L(x_n)\to L(x)\). Hence

\[ \|x\|_X=L(x)=\lim_{n\to\infty}L(x_n). \]

For every \(n\),

\[ L(x_n)\le |L(x_n)|\le \|L\|_{X^*}\|x_n\|_X=\|x_n\|_X. \]

Taking liminf gives

\[ \|x\|_X\le\liminf_{n\to\infty}\|x_n\|_X. \]
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 2. Weak Convergence, Weak-Star Convergence, And Banach-Alaoglu
questions.questionHtml: Show that if \(x_n\) weakly converges to \(x\) and \(L_n\) converges to \(L\) in \(X^*\), then \(L_n(x_n)\to L(x)\) as \(n\to\infty\).
questions.strategyHtml: The problem wants the proof strategy before the details. Identify the hypotheses, translate the desired conclusion into the right definition, choose the standard tool, and close by verifying the final limit, estimate, or identity.
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ |L_n(x_n) - L(x)| = |L_n(x_n) - L(x_n) + L(x_n) - L(x)| \le |(L_n - L)(x_n)| + |L(x_n) - L(x)| \]
\[ \le \|L_n - L\|_{X^*} \|x_n\|_X + |L(x_n) - L(x)| \le \|L_n - L\|_{X^*} M + |L(x_n) - L(x)|. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: For weak questions, never test vectors directly; test them through every continuous linear functional.

Let \(x_n \rightharpoonup x\) in \(X\) and \(L_n \to L\) strongly in \(X^*\). Since \((x_n)\) is weakly convergent, it is norm-bounded, so there exists \(M > 0\) such that \(\|x_n\|_X \le M\) for all \(n \ge 1\). We decompose the difference as:

\[ |L_n(x_n) - L(x)| = |L_n(x_n) - L(x_n) + L(x_n) - L(x)| \le |(L_n - L)(x_n)| + |L(x_n) - L(x)| \]
\[ \le \|L_n - L\|_{X^*} \|x_n\|_X + |L(x_n) - L(x)| \le \|L_n - L\|_{X^*} M + |L(x_n) - L(x)|. \]
Since \(L_n \to L\) strongly, \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \|L_n - L\|_{X^*} M = 0\). Since \(x_n \rightharpoonup x\), \(\lim_{n\to\infty} |L(x_n) - L(x)| = 0\). Thus, \(\lim_{n\to\infty} |L_n(x_n) - L(x)| = 0\), which proves \(L_n(x_n) \to L(x)\).
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 2. Weak Convergence, Weak-Star Convergence, And Banach-Alaoglu
questions.questionHtml: Show that \(T\in L(X,Y)\) is weak-weak continuous.
questions.strategyHtml: The proof is a composition test. To show weak convergence of \(Tx_n\), fix a functional on the target, compose it with \(T\), and use weak convergence of \(x_n\) in the source.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Bounded linear maps commute with weak convergence by testing after composition with dual functionals.

Let \(T \in L(X, Y)\) be a bounded linear operator, and let \(x_n \rightharpoonup x\) weakly in \(X\). We show \(Tx_n \rightharpoonup Tx\) weakly in \(Y\). Let \(M \in Y^*\) be any continuous linear functional on \(Y\). Define the composition \(L = M \circ T\). Since \(T\) is bounded and linear, and \(M\) is continuous and linear, their composition \(L\) is a continuous linear functional on \(X\), i.e., \(L \in X^*\). Since \(x_n \rightharpoonup x\), we have:

\[ \lim_{n\to\infty} L(x_n) = L(x) \implies \lim_{n\to\infty} M(Tx_n) = M(Tx). \]
Since this holds for every \(M \in Y^*\), the sequence \((Tx_n)\) converges weakly to \(Tx\) in \(Y\).
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 2. Weak Convergence, Weak-Star Convergence, And Banach-Alaoglu
questions.questionHtml: Write the definition of weak* convergence.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Weak-star tests only against the predual; weak tests against the whole dual.

Let \(X\) be a normed space and \(X^*\) its dual space. A sequence \((L_n)\) in \(X^*\) converges weak-star to \(L \in X^*\) (denoted by \(L_n \overset{*}{\rightharpoonup} L\)) if:

\[ \lim_{n\to\infty} L_n(x) = L(x) \qquad \forall x \in X. \]
Unlike weak convergence on \(X^*\) (which requires convergence when tested against all functionals in \(X^{**}\)), weak-star convergence only requires convergence when tested against the canonical images of elements of \(X\).
questions.finalReason: repeated core ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 2. Weak Convergence, Weak-Star Convergence, And Banach-Alaoglu
questions.questionHtml: Write the properties of weak* convergence.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Weak-star tests only against the predual; weak tests against the whole dual.

Let \(X\) be a normed space. Weak-star convergence in \(X^*\) satisfies: 1. Uniqueness of Limits: If \(L_n \overset{*}{\rightharpoonup} L\) and \(L_n \overset{*}{\rightharpoonup} M\), then \(L = M\) (since the predual \(X\) separates points of \(X^*\)). 2. Boundedness: If \(X\) is a Banach space, then any weak-star convergent sequence \((L_n)\) in \(X^*\) is norm-bounded: \(\sup_{n\ge 1} \|L_n\|_{X^*} < \infty\) (by the Uniform Boundedness Principle). 3. Lower Semicontinuity of the Norm: If \(L_n \overset{*}{\rightharpoonup} L\), then:

\[ \|L\|_{X^*} \le \liminf_{n\to\infty} \|L_n\|_{X^*}. \]
Proof: For any \(x \in X\), \(|L(x)| = \lim |L_n(x)| = \liminf |L_n(x)| \le \liminf (\|L_n\|_{X^*} \|x\|_X) = \left(\liminf \|L_n\|_{X^*}\right) \|x\|_X\). Taking the supremum over \(\|x\|_X \le 1\) yields the result. 4. Linearity: If \(L_n \overset{*}{\rightharpoonup} L\) and \(M_n \overset{*}{\rightharpoonup} M\), then \(\alpha L_n + \beta M_n \overset{*}{\rightharpoonup} \alpha L + \beta M\) for all scalars \(\alpha, \beta\).
questions.finalReason: repeated core ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 2. Weak Convergence, Weak-Star Convergence, And Banach-Alaoglu
questions.questionHtml: Which is the relation between weak and weak* convergence? Justify your answer.
questions.strategyHtml: The proof is compactness through the correct topology. Identify weak-star convergence as pointwise convergence on the predual, use Banach-Alaoglu or metrizability on bounded sets, and then translate compactness into a subsequence statement.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Weak-star tests only against the predual; weak tests against the whole dual.

Let \(X\) be a normed space and \(X^*\) its dual. Weak convergence in \(X^*\) implies weak-star convergence. The converse holds if and only if \(X\) is reflexive. - Justification (Weak implies Weak-star): Let \((L_n)\) be a sequence in \(X^*\) converging weakly to \(L \in X^*\). This means that for every \(\Phi \in X^{**}\), \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \Phi(L_n) = \Phi(L)\). Let \(\tau : X \to X^{**}\) be the canonical evaluation map. For any \(x \in X\), we have \(\tau(x) \in X^{**}\). Testing the weak convergence against \(\tau(x)\) gives:

\[ \lim_{n\to\infty} \tau(x)(L_n) = \tau(x)(L) \implies \lim_{n\to\infty} L_n(x) = L(x), \]
which is the definition of weak-star convergence \(L_n \overset{*}{\rightharpoonup} L\). - Counterexample for the Converse (Non-reflexive case): Let \(X = c_0\) (the space of real sequences converging to 0, with supremum norm). Its dual is \(X^* = \ell^1\), and its bidual is \(X^{**} = \ell^\infty\). Let \((e_n)_{n=1}^\infty \subset \ell^1\) be the standard basis. For any \(x \in c_0\), \(e_n(x) = x(n) \to 0\) as \(n\to\infty\). Thus \(e_n \overset{*}{\rightharpoonup} 0\) weak-star in \ell^1. However, to converge weakly to 0, it must satisfy \(\Phi(e_n) \to 0\) for all \(\Phi \in \ell^\infty\). Choosing \(y = (1, 1, 1, \dots) \in \ell^\infty\) defines a functional \(\Phi_y \in X^{**}\) such that \(\Phi_y(e_n) = y(n) = 1 \not\to 0\). Thus \((e_n)\) does not converge weakly to 0.
questions.finalReason: repeated core ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 2. Weak Convergence, Weak-Star Convergence, And Banach-Alaoglu
questions.questionHtml: State the Banach-Alaoglu theorem.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Banach-Alaoglu compactness is weak-star compactness of the dual unit ball.

The closed unit ball of the dual space \(X^*\) of any normed space \(X\), defined as \(\overline{B}_{X^*} = \{ L \in X^* : \|L\|_{X^*} \le 1 \}\), is compact in the weak-star topology \(\sigma(X^*, X)\). - Metrizability and Sequential Compactness: If \(X\) is a separable normed space, the weak-star topology on the closed unit ball \(\overline{B}_{X^*}\) (and on any bounded subset of \(X^*\)) is metrizable. Since compact metric spaces are sequentially compact, it follows that when \(X\) is separable, any bounded sequence in \(X^*\) contains a weak-star convergent subsequence.
questions.finalReason: July exact history; repeated core ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 2. Weak Convergence, Weak-Star Convergence, And Banach-Alaoglu
questions.questionHtml: Why can we say that from a bounded sequence in \(L^\infty\) we can extract a subsequence which weakly* converges in \(L^\infty\)?
questions.strategyHtml: The proof is compactness through the correct topology. Identify weak-star convergence as pointwise convergence on the predual, use Banach-Alaoglu or metrizability on bounded sets, and then translate compactness into a subsequence statement.
questions.keyStepsHtml: \[ \lim_{k\to\infty} \int_X f_{n_k} g \, d\mu = \int_X fg \, d\mu \qquad \forall g \in L^1(X, \mu). \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Banach-Alaoglu compactness is weak-star compactness of the dual unit ball.

The space \(L^\infty(X, \mu)\) on a separable, \(\sigma\)-finite measure space is the dual space of \(L^1(X, \mu)\), which is a separable Banach space. According to the Banach-Alaoglu Theorem, the closed unit ball of the dual space \((L^1)^*\) is weak-star compact. Furthermore, because the predual space \(L^1(X, \mu)\) is separable, the weak-star topology restricted to any bounded ball of \(L^\infty(X, \mu)\) is metrizable. Since compact metric spaces are sequentially compact, any bounded sequence \((f_n)\) in \(L^\infty(X, \mu)\) lies within a weak-star compact metric subspace. Thus, there exists a subsequence \((f_{n_k})\) and a function \(f \in L^\infty(X, \mu)\) such that \(f_{n_k} \overset{*}{\rightharpoonup} f\), which means:

\[ \lim_{k\to\infty} \int_X f_{n_k} g \, d\mu = \int_X fg \, d\mu \qquad \forall g \in L^1(X, \mu). \]
questions.finalReason: July exact history; repeated core ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 2. Weak Convergence, Weak-Star Convergence, And Banach-Alaoglu
questions.questionHtml: State and prove the corollary of the Banach-Alaoglu theorem in a separable and reflexive Banach space.
questions.strategyHtml: The proof is compactness through the correct topology. Identify weak-star convergence as pointwise convergence on the predual, use Banach-Alaoglu or metrizability on bounded sets, and then translate compactness into a subsequence statement.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Banach-Alaoglu compactness is weak-star compactness of the dual unit ball.

Corollary: Let \(X\) be a reflexive Banach space. Any bounded sequence \((x_n) \subset X\) contains a weakly convergent subsequence \((x_{n_k})\).

Proof (Separable Reduction): Let \((x_n)\) be a bounded sequence in \(X\) with \(\|x_n\|_X \le M\) for all \(n \ge 1\). Define \(Y = \overline{\operatorname{span}}\{x_n : n \ge 1\}\). Since \(Y\) is a closed subspace of the reflexive space \(X\), \(Y\) is reflexive. Being the closure of the span of a countable set, \(Y\) is separable. Since \(Y\) is reflexive, the canonical map \(\tau : Y \to Y^{**}\) is an isometric isomorphism, meaning \(Y^{**}\) is also separable, which implies that the dual space \(Y^*\) is separable. Consider the sequence of canonical images \(\tau(x_n) \in Y^{**} = (Y^*)^*\). Since \(\|\tau(x_n)\|_{Y^{**}} = \|x_n\|_Y \le M\), this is a bounded sequence in the dual of the separable space \(Y^*\). By the Banach-Alaoglu Theorem, the weak-star topology on the closed unit ball of \(Y^{**}\) is metrizable and compact, hence sequentially compact. Thus, there exists a subsequence \(\tau(x_{n_k})\) and \(\Phi \in Y^{**}\) such that \(\tau(x_{n_k}) \overset{*}{\rightharpoonup} \Phi\) weak-star in \(Y^{**}\). Since \(Y\) is reflexive, there exists \(x \in Y\) such that \(\tau(x) = \Phi\). Evaluating the weak-star convergence on any \(L \in Y^*\) yields:

\[ \lim_{k\to\infty} L(x_{n_k}) = \lim_{k\to\infty} \tau(x_{n_k})(L) = \tau(x)(L) = L(x). \]
This proves \(x_{n_k} \rightharpoonup x\) weakly in \(Y\). By the Hahn-Banach Theorem, every continuous functional on \(X\) restricts to a continuous functional on \(Y\), so \(x_{n_k} \rightharpoonup x\) weakly in \(X\) as well.
questions.finalReason: July exact history; repeated core ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Write the definition of compact operator.
questions.answerHtml: A linear operator \(T : X \to Y\) between normed spaces \(X\) and \(Y\) is compact if for every bounded subset \(B \subset X\), the image \(T(B)\) is relatively compact in \(Y\) (i.e., its closure \(\overline{T(B)}\) is compact in \(Y\)). Equivalently, since \(Y\) is a metric space, \(T\) is compact if and only if for every bounded sequence \((x_n) \subset X\), the sequence \((Tx_n)\) contains a subsequence that converges strongly (in norm) in \(Y\). Every compact linear operator is automatically bounded (continuous).
questions.finalReason: exact Jan/Feb-2026 ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Write the definition of operator of finite rank.
questions.keyStepsHtml: \[ Tx = \sum_{j=1}^m L_j(x) y_j \qquad \forall x \in X, \]
questions.answerHtml:
A bounded linear operator \(T : X \to Y\) between normed spaces is of finite rank if the range of \(T\) is finite-dimensional, i.e., \(\operatorname{dim}(T(X)) < \infty\). Any bounded linear operator of finite rank can be written in the form:

\[ Tx = \sum_{j=1}^m L_j(x) y_j \qquad \forall x \in X, \]
where \(\{y_1, \dots, y_m\}\) is a basis of \(\operatorname{Ran}(T) \subset Y\) and \(L_1, \dots, L_m \in X^*\) are continuous linear functionals.
questions.finalReason: exact Jan/Feb-2026 ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: How is a compact operator related to operators of finite rank?
questions.strategyHtml: The proof should connect compactness with subsequences. Use boundedness to get weakly convergent subsequences when needed, apply compactness to get strong subsequences, and compare limits to identify the strong limit.
questions.answerHtml:
Every bounded linear operator of finite rank is compact. This is because the image of any bounded set \(B \subset X\) is bounded in the finite-dimensional space \(\operatorname{Ran}(T)\). By the Heine-Borel theorem, bounded subsets of finite-dimensional normed spaces are relatively compact. The converse is false in infinite-dimensional spaces. - Counterexample: Let \(T : \ell^2 \to \ell^2\) be the diagonal operator defined by \(T(x_n) = (x_n / n)\). Its range is infinite-dimensional, so \(T\) is not of finite rank. We show \(T\) is compact by expressing it as the operator-norm limit of finite-rank operators \(T_N\) defined by \(T_N(x) = (x_1, x_2/2, \dots, x_N/N, 0, 0, \dots)\). The difference satisfies:

\[ \|T - T_N\|_{L(\ell^2)} = \sup_{\|x\|_2 \le 1} \left( \sum_{k=N+1}^\infty \frac{|x_k|^2}{k^2} \right)^{1/2} \le \frac{1}{N+1} \to 0 \quad \text{as } N\to\infty. \]
Since the space of compact operators is closed under the operator norm topology, the limit operator \(T\) is compact.
questions.finalReason: exact Jan/Feb-2026 ID; repeated core ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: State and prove the theorem about the characterization of compact operators in terms of weak-strong continuity.
questions.strategyHtml: The proof should connect compactness with subsequences. Use boundedness to get weakly convergent subsequences when needed, apply compactness to get strong subsequences, and compare limits to identify the strong limit.
questions.keyStepsHtml: \[ x_n \rightharpoonup x \text{ weakly in } X \implies Tx_n \to Tx \text{ strongly in } Y. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be Banach spaces with \(X\) reflexive. A bounded linear operator \(T : X \to Y\) is compact if and only if it maps weakly convergent sequences in \(X\) to strongly convergent sequences in \(Y\), i.e.,

\[ x_n \rightharpoonup x \text{ weakly in } X \implies Tx_n \to Tx \text{ strongly in } Y. \]
- Proof (Forward Direction): Let \(T\) be compact, and let \(x_n \rightharpoonup x\) weakly in \(X\). Since \(T\) is weak-weak continuous, \(Tx_n \rightharpoonup Tx\) weakly in \(Y\). We show \(Tx_n \to Tx\) strongly. Suppose for contradiction that \(Tx_n \not\to Tx\) strongly. Then there exists \(\varepsilon > 0\) and a subsequence \((x_{n_k})\) such that \(\|Tx_{n_k} - Tx\|_Y \ge \varepsilon\) for all \(k \ge 1\). Since \((x_n)\) is weakly convergent, it is norm-bounded, so the subsequence \((x_{n_k})\) is also bounded. Since \(T\) is compact, \((Tx_{n_k})\) contains a subsequence \((Tx_{n_{k_j}})\) that converges strongly to some \(y \in Y\). Strong convergence implies weak convergence, so \(Tx_{n_{k_j}} \rightharpoonup y\) weakly. Uniqueness of weak limits forces \(y = Tx\). Thus \(Tx_{n_{k_j}} \to Tx\) strongly, contradicting \(\|Tx_{n_{k_j}} - Tx\|_Y \ge \varepsilon\). Thus, \(Tx_n \to Tx\) strongly. - Proof (Backward Direction): Assume \(T\) maps weak convergence to strong convergence. Let \((x_n)\) be any bounded sequence in \(X\). Since \(X\) is reflexive, the Banach-Alaoglu corollary implies there exists a subsequence \((x_{n_k})\) that converges weakly to some \(x \in X\). By hypothesis, this implies \(Tx_{n_k} \to Tx\) strongly in \(Y\). Thus, the bounded sequence has a strongly convergent subsequence, so \(T\) is compact.
questions.finalReason: exact Jan/Feb-2026 ID; not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Can a compact operator be surjective? Justify your answer.
questions.strategyHtml: The problem wants a sharp yes/no answer, not just a slogan. Decide the claimed implication, then either prove it from the definitions or choose a standard model example, verify the stated convergence/property, and explicitly show the missing conclusion fails.
questions.answerHtml: A compact linear operator \(T : X \to Y\) between Banach spaces cannot be surjective if \(Y\) is infinite-dimensional. - Proof: Suppose for contradiction that \(T\) is surjective. By the Open Mapping Theorem, \(T\) is an open mapping. Thus, there exists \(r > 0\) such that the closed ball \(\overline{B}_Y(0, r) \subset \overline{T(B_X(0,1))}\). Since \(T\) is compact, the set \(\overline{T(B_X(0,1))}\) is compact, which implies that the closed ball \(\overline{B}_Y(0, r)\) is compact. By Riesz's Theorem, the closed unit ball of a normed space is compact if and only if the space is finite-dimensional. This implies \(\operatorname{dim}(Y) < \infty\), which is a contradiction.
questions.finalReason: exact Jan/Feb-2026 ID; repeated core ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: State the Ascoli-Arzela theorem.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Ascoli-Arzela is compactness from uniform boundedness plus equicontinuity.

Let \(K\) be a compact metric space. A subset \(\mathcal{F} \subset C^0(K)\) is relatively compact in the supremum norm if and only if it is: 1. Equibounded: There exists \(M > 0\) such that \(|f(t)| \le M\) for all \(f \in \mathcal{F}\) and all \(t \in K\). 2. Equicontinuous: For every \(\varepsilon > 0\), there exists \(\delta > 0\) such that for all \(s, t \in K\):

\[ d_K(s, t) < \delta \implies |f(s) - f(t)| < \varepsilon \qquad \forall f \in \mathcal{F}. \]
questions.finalReason: not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Write the statement of the Ascoli-Arzela theorem when the subset of \(C^0(X)\) is a sequence \(\{f_n\}\). Is there any sufficient condition to apply the theorem?
questions.keyStepsHtml: \[ |f_n(s) - f_n(t)| \le L |s - t| \qquad \forall s, t \in [a, b], \, \forall n \ge 1, \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: For sequences in \(C^0([a,b])\), uniform boundedness plus equicontinuity gives a uniformly convergent subsequence.

Ascoli-Arzelà for sequences. Let \((f_n)\subset C^0([a,b])\). If \((f_n)\) is uniformly bounded and equicontinuous, then there exist a subsequence \((f_{n_k})\) and a function \(f\in C^0([a,b])\) such that

\[ \|f_{n_k}-f\|_\infty\to0. \]

Uniform boundedness means that there exists \(M>0\) such that \(\sup_{[a,b]}|f_n|\le M\) for every \(n\). Equicontinuity means that for every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists \(\delta>0\), independent of \(n\), such that

\[ |x-y|<\delta\quad\Longrightarrow\quad |f_n(x)-f_n(y)|<\varepsilon\qquad\forall n. \]

A standard sufficient condition is boundedness in \(C^1([a,b])\): if

\[ \sup_n\|f_n\|_\infty\le C, \qquad \sup_n\|f_n'\|_\infty\le C, \]

then the sequence is uniformly bounded and, by the Mean Value Theorem,

\[ |f_n(x)-f_n(y)|\le C|x-y|\qquad\forall x,y\in[a,b],\ \forall n. \]

Thus the sequence is equi-Lipschitz, hence equicontinuous, and Ascoli-Arzelà applies.
questions.finalReason: not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 4. Hilbert Spaces, Fredholm, Spectrum, And Spectral Theorem
questions.questionHtml: State and prove the projection theorem and its corollary in Hilbert spaces.
questions.strategyHtml: The argument is geometric. Minimize the distance to the closed convex set, use Hilbert-space parallelogram/convexity to get existence and uniqueness, then characterize the minimizer by an orthogonality inequality.
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ \|x-p\|_2 = \inf_{y \in K} \|x-y\|_2. \]
\[ \operatorname{Re}\langle x-p, y-p \rangle \le 0 \qquad \forall y \in K. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Projection theorem is nearest point plus variational inequality; for subspaces it becomes orthogonality.

Projection Theorem. Let \(H\) be a Hilbert space and let \(K\subset H\) be nonempty, closed, and convex. For every \(f\in H\), there exists a unique \(u\in K\) such that

\[ \|f-u\|=\min_{v\in K}\|f-v\|=\operatorname{dist}(f,K). \]

Moreover, \(u\) is this minimizer if and only if \(u\in K\) and

\[ \langle f-u,v-u\rangle\le0\qquad \forall v\in K \]

in the real case; in the complex case use \(\operatorname{Re}\langle f-u,v-u\rangle\le0\).

Existence. Let \((v_n)\subset K\) be a minimizing sequence and set \(d_n=\|f-v_n\|\to d:=\inf_{v\in K}\|f-v\|\). By the parallelogram law,

\[ \left\|f-{v_n+v_m\over2}\right\|^2+\left\|{v_n-v_m\over2}\right\|^2={1\over2}(d_n^2+d_m^2). \]

Since \((v_n+v_m)/2\in K\), the first term is at least \(d^2\), hence \(\|v_n-v_m\|\to0\). Thus \((v_n)\) is Cauchy. Completeness of \(H\) gives \(v_n\to u\in H\), and closedness of \(K\) gives \(u\in K\). Passing to the limit gives \(\|f-u\|=d\).

Uniqueness. If \(u,\tilde u\in K\) are two minimizers, applying the same identity to \(f-u\) and \(f-\tilde u\) and using \((u+\tilde u)/2\in K\) gives \(\|u-\tilde u\|=0\), so \(u=\tilde u\).

Corollary for closed subspaces. If \(M\subset H\) is a closed vector subspace, then \(u=P_M f\) if and only if

\[ u\in M, \qquad \langle f-u,v\rangle=0\quad\forall v\in M. \]

Indeed, the variational inequality applied to \(u+tv\in M\), with arbitrary real \(t\), forces \(\langle f-u,v\rangle=0\). Conversely, this orthogonality implies \(\langle f-u,\xi-u\rangle=0\le0\) for every \(\xi\in M\), so \(u\) is the projection.
questions.finalReason: repeated core ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 4. Hilbert Spaces, Fredholm, Spectrum, And Spectral Theorem
questions.questionHtml: Write the definition of symmetric operator on Hilbert spaces.
questions.keyStepsHtml: \[ \langle Tx, y \rangle = \langle x, Ty \rangle \qquad \forall x, y \in H. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: For symmetric operators, the quadratic form controls the norm.

A bounded linear operator \(T : H \to H\) on a Hilbert space \(H\) is symmetric (or self-adjoint) if:

\[ \langle Tx, y \rangle = \langle x, Ty \rangle \qquad \forall x, y \in H. \]
In the complex setting, this is equivalent to \(T = T^*\), where the adjoint is defined with respect to the complex inner product.
questions.finalReason: July exact history; repeated core ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 4. Hilbert Spaces, Fredholm, Spectrum, And Spectral Theorem
questions.questionHtml: How is it possible to compute the norm of a symmetric operator?
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: For symmetric operators, the quadratic form controls the norm.

If \(T \in L(H)\) is a self-adjoint operator on a Hilbert space \(H\), then:

\[ \|T\| = \sup_{\|x\| \le 1} |\langle Tx, x \rangle|. \]
This means the operator norm is exactly the supremum of its quadratic form on the unit sphere.
questions.finalReason: July exact history; repeated core ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 4. Hilbert Spaces, Fredholm, Spectrum, And Spectral Theorem
questions.questionHtml: State the Fredholm alternative.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Fredholm alternative: either the homogeneous problem has only zero and every f is solvable, or solvability is exactly an orthogonality condition.

Let \(H\) be a Hilbert space and let \(T\in K(H)\) be compact and symmetric. For the equation

\[ u-Tu=f, \]

exactly one of the following alternatives holds.

  1. If \(\ker(I-T)=\{0\}\), then \(I-T\) is injective and hence surjective. Therefore for every \(f\in H\) there exists a unique \(u\in H\) solving \(u-Tu=f\).
  2. If \(\ker(I-T)\ne\{0\}\), then the equation is solvable if and only if
\[ f\in\ker(I-T)^\perp. \]

When a solution exists in this second case, it is not unique: one may add any element of \(\ker(I-T)\) to a particular solution.

The key Fredholm facts behind this are: \(\dim\ker(I-T)<\infty\), \(\operatorname{Im}(I-T)\) is closed, and for symmetric \(T\),

\[ \operatorname{Im}(I-T)=\ker(I-T)^\perp. \]
questions.finalReason: July exact history; repeated core ID

The key Fredholm facts behind this are: \(\dim\ker(I-T)<\infty\), \(\operatorname{Im}(I-T)\) is closed, and for symmetric \(T\),

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 4. Hilbert Spaces, Fredholm, Spectrum, And Spectral Theorem
questions.questionHtml: Write the definitions of \(\rho(T)\), \(\sigma(T)\), \(EV(T)\), \(\sigma_p(T)\).
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ \rho(T) = \{ \lambda \in \mathbb{C} : \lambda I - T \text{ is bijective and } (\lambda I - T)^{-1} \in L(X) \}. \]
\[ \sigma_p(T) = \{ \lambda \in \mathbb{C} : \lambda I - T \text{ is not injective} \} = \{ \lambda \in \mathbb{C} : \ker(\lambda I - T) \neq \{0\} \}. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Compact spectrum is zero plus isolated finite-multiplicity eigenvalues accumulating only at zero.

Let \(T : X \to X\) be a bounded linear operator on a Banach space \(X\). 1. Resolvent Set \(\rho(T)\):

\[ \rho(T) = \{ \lambda \in \mathbb{C} : \lambda I - T \text{ is bijective and } (\lambda I - T)^{-1} \in L(X) \}. \]
2. Spectrum \(\sigma(T)\): The complement of the resolvent set, \(\sigma(T) = \mathbb{C} \setminus \rho(T)\). It is partitioned into: - Point Spectrum \(\sigma_p(T)\) / Eigenvalues \(EV(T)\):
\[ \sigma_p(T) = \{ \lambda \in \mathbb{C} : \lambda I - T \text{ is not injective} \} = \{ \lambda \in \mathbb{C} : \ker(\lambda I - T) \neq \{0\} \}. \]
- Continuous Spectrum \(\sigma_c(T)\): \(\lambda I - T\) is injective and has dense but non-surjective range. - Residual Spectrum \(\sigma_r(T)\): \(\lambda I - T\) is injective and its range is not dense in \(X\).
questions.finalReason: July exact history; repeated core ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 4. Hilbert Spaces, Fredholm, Spectrum, And Spectral Theorem
questions.questionHtml: What is the structure of the spectrum of a compact operator?
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Compact spectrum is zero plus isolated finite-multiplicity eigenvalues accumulating only at zero.

Let \(E\) be an infinite-dimensional Banach space and let \(T\in K(E)\). Then the spectrum of \(T\) has the following structure.

  1. \(0\in\sigma(T)\).
  2. Every nonzero spectral value is an eigenvalue:
\[ \sigma(T)\setminus\{0\}=EV(T)\setminus\{0\}. \]
  1. The nonzero spectrum is either empty, finite, or a sequence converging to \(0\). Equivalently, the only possible accumulation point of \(\sigma(T)\) is \(0\).

Moreover, each nonzero eigenvalue has finite-dimensional eigenspace. Thus compact operators cannot have a large continuous nonzero spectrum; away from \(0\), the spectrum is made of isolated eigenvalues.
questions.finalReason: July exact history; repeated core ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.chapter: 4. Hilbert Spaces, Fredholm, Spectrum, And Spectral Theorem
questions.questionHtml: State the spectral theorem.
questions.keyStepsHtml: \[ Tx = \sum_n \lambda_n \langle x, e_n \rangle e_n \qquad \forall x \in H. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Compact symmetric operators on a separable Hilbert space diagonalize in an orthonormal eigenbasis.

Spectral Theorem. Let \(H\) be a separable Hilbert space and let \(T\in K(H)\) be a symmetric compact linear operator, i.e.

\[ \langle Tx,y\rangle=\langle x,Ty\rangle\qquad \forall x,y\in H. \]

Then there exists an orthonormal basis of \(H\) made of eigenvectors of \(T\). Equivalently, \(T\) is diagonal with respect to a Hilbert basis: if \((e_k)\) is that basis, then

\[ T e_k=\lambda_k e_k, \]

with real eigenvalues \(\lambda_k\). Compactness is essential for this statement in this form; without compactness, a symmetric operator need not have an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors.
questions.finalReason: July exact history; repeated core ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Write the definition of conjugate numbers. Show the Young inequality.
questions.strategyHtml: Normalize first so the inequality becomes dimensionless. Prove the normalized estimate with Young inequality, then rescale; equality and zero cases should be handled before the normalization.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Young is the scalar inequality behind Holder: ab <= a^p/p + b^q/q.

Conjugate exponents \(p, q \in (1, \infty)\) satisfy:

\[ \frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1. \]
Young's inequality states that for all \(a, b \ge 0\):
\[ ab \le \frac{a^p}{p} + \frac{b^q}{q}. \]
Proof: If \(a = 0\) or \(b = 0\), the inequality holds trivially. Assume \(a > 0\) and \(b > 0\). Since the natural logarithm function \(\ln(x)\) is strictly concave on \((0, \infty)\), we have for \(\theta = 1/p \in (0, 1)\) and \(u = a^p, v = b^q\):
\[ \ln(\theta u + (1-\theta)v) \ge \theta \ln(u) + (1-\theta)\ln(v) \implies \ln\left( \frac{a^p}{p} + \frac{b^q}{q} \right) \ge \frac{1}{p} \ln(a^p) + \frac{1}{q} \ln(b^q) = \ln(ab). \]
Since the exponential function is strictly increasing, applying it to both sides yields \(ab \le \frac{a^p}{p} + \frac{b^q}{q}\).
questions.finalReason: not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Write and show the Holder inequality.
questions.strategyHtml: Normalize first so the inequality becomes dimensionless. Prove the normalized estimate with Young inequality, then rescale; equality and zero cases should be handled before the normalization.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Holder is Young after normalizing ||f||p and ||g||q to one.

Hölder's Inequality: Let \(1 < p, q < \infty\) with \(\frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1\). If \(f \in L^p(X, \mu)\) and \(g \in L^q(X, \mu)\), then \(fg \in L^1(X, \mu)\) and:

\[ \int_X |fg| \, d\mu \le \|f\|_p \|g\|_q. \]
Proof: If \(\|f\|_p = 0\) or \(\|g\|_q = 0\), then \(f = 0\) a.e. or \(g = 0\) a.e. In this case, \(fg = 0\) a.e., so both sides of the inequality are \(0\), and the result holds. Assume \(\|f\|_p > 0\) and \(\|g\|_q > 0\). For a.e. \(x \in X\), apply Young's inequality pointwise to \(a(x) = \frac{|f(x)|}{\|f\|_p}\) and \(b(x) = \frac{|g(x)|}{\|g\|_q}\):
\[ \frac{|f(x)g(x)|}{\|f\|_p \|g\|_q} \le \frac{|f(x)|^p}{p \|f\|_p^p} + \frac{|g(x)|^q}{q \|g\|_q^q}. \]
Integrating both sides over \(X\) yields:
\[ \int_X \frac{|fg|}{\|f\|_p \|g\|_q} \, d\mu \le \frac{1}{p \|f\|_p^p} \int_X |f|^p \, d\mu + \frac{1}{q \|g\|_q^q} \int_X |g|^q \, d\mu = \frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1. \]
Multiplying by \(\|f\|_p \|g\|_q\) yields the inequality.
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Write and show the Minkowski inequality.
questions.strategyHtml: The proof is the triangle inequality for \(L^p\). Split \(|f+g|^p\), apply Holder to each term, cancel the common factor when nonzero, and treat the zero case separately.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Minkowski is the triangle inequality for Lp; prove it by Holder on |f+g|^{p-1}.

Minkowski's Inequality: For \(1 \le p \le \infty\) and \(f, g \in L^p(X, \mu)\):

\[ \|f+g\|_p \le \|f\|_p + \|g\|_p. \]
Proof: If \(p = 1\), the triangle inequality on \(\mathbb{K}\) yields \(|f+g| \le |f| + |g|\). Integrating yields the result. If \(p = \infty\), since \(|f| \le \|f\|_\infty\) a.e. and \(|g| \le \|g\|_\infty\) a.e., we have \(|f+g| \le \|f\|_\infty + \|g\|_\infty\) a.e., which yields \(\|f+g\|_\infty \le \|f\|_\infty + \|g\|_\infty\). Now let \(1 < p < \infty\), and let \(q = \frac{p}{p-1}\) be the conjugate exponent. We have:
\[ |f+g|^p \le (|f| + |g|) |f+g|^{p-1} = |f| |f+g|^{p-1} + |g| |f+g|^{p-1}. \]
Integrating both sides and applying Hölder's inequality to each term:
\[ \int_X |f+g|^p \, d\mu \le \|f\|_p \left( \int_X |f+g|^{(p-1)q} \, d\mu \right)^{1/q} + \|g\|_p \left( \int_X |f+g|^{(p-1)q} \, d\mu \right)^{1/q}. \]
Since \((p-1)q = p\), this simplifies to:
\[ \int_X |f+g|^p \, d\mu \le (\|f\|_p + \|g\|_p) \left(\int_X |f+g|^p \, d\mu\right)^{1/q}. \]
If \(\|f+g\|_p = 0\), the inequality is trivial. If \(\|f+g\|_p > 0\), we divide both sides by \(\left(\int_X |f+g|^p \, d\mu\right)^{1/q}\) (using \(1 - 1/q = 1/p\)) to obtain \(\|f+g\|_p \le \|f\|_p + \|g\|_p\).
questions.finalReason: not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Show that \(L^p\) is a normed space.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Lp is normed because zero norm means zero a.e., homogeneity is algebra, and triangle inequality is Minkowski.

For \(1\le p<\infty\), define

\[ \|f\|_p:=\left(\int_X|f|^p\,d\mu\right)^{1/p}, \]

and for \(p=\infty\), \(\|f\|_\infty:=\operatorname*{esssup}_X|f|\). This is well-defined on equivalence classes in \(L^p\).

The norm axioms are as follows.

  1. Definiteness. \(\|f\|_p\ge0\), and \(\|f\|_p=0\) iff \(|f|^p=0\) a.e., iff \(f=0\) in \(L^p\). For \(p=\infty\), \(\operatorname*{esssup}|f|=0\) also means \(f=0\) a.e.
  2. Homogeneity. \(\|\alpha f\|_p=|\alpha|\|f\|_p\), directly from \(|\alpha f|^p=|\alpha|^p|f|^p\), and similarly for \(p=\infty\).
  3. Triangle inequality. \(\|f+g\|_p\le\|f\|_p+\|g\|_p\) is exactly Minkowski's inequality. For \(p=1\) it follows by integrating \(|f+g|\le|f|+|g|\); for \(1Holder's inequality.

Therefore \(L^p(X)\) is a normed space.
questions.finalReason: not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Show the completeness of \(L^p\) spaces.
questions.strategyHtml: Use the standard Cauchy-series strategy. Extract a rapidly Cauchy subsequence, dominate the telescoping sum by an \(L^p\) function, get an a.e. limit, then prove the whole Cauchy sequence converges to it in norm.
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ \int_X g^p \, d\mu = \lim_{m\to\infty} \int_X g_m^p \, d\mu \le 1. \]
\[ \int_X |f_{n_k} - f|^p \, d\mu = \int_X \liminf_{j\to\infty} |f_{n_k} - f_{n_j}|^p \, d\mu \le \liminf_{j\to\infty} \int_X |f_{n_k} - f_{n_j}|^p \, d\mu. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Completeness of Lp comes from a summable subsequence and a.e. limit reconstruction.

This is the Riesz-Fischer theorem.

Case 1: \(1 \le p < \infty\). Let \((f_n)\) be a Cauchy sequence in \(L^p(X, \mu)\). We extract a subsequence \((f_{n_k})\) such that \(\|f_{n_{k+1}} - f_{n_k}\|_p \le 2^{-k}\) for all \(k \ge 1\). Define \(g_m(x) = \sum_{k=1}^m |f_{n_{k+1}}(x) - f_{n_k}(x)|\) and \(g(x) = \sum_{k=1}^\infty |f_{n_{k+1}}(x) - f_{n_k}(x)|\). By Minkowski's inequality, \(\|g_m\|_p \le \sum_{k=1}^m \|f_{n_{k+1}} - f_{n_k}\|_p \le \sum_{k=1}^m 2^{-k} < 1\). Applying the Monotone Convergence Theorem to \((g_m^p)\):

\[ \int_X g^p \, d\mu = \lim_{m\to\infty} \int_X g_m^p \, d\mu \le 1. \]
Thus \(g \in L^p(X, \mu)\), which implies \(g(x) < \infty\) for a.e. \(x \in X\). This means the telescopic series \(f_{n_1}(x) + \sum_{k=1}^\infty (f_{n_{k+1}}(x) - f_{n_k}(x))\) converges absolutely for a.e. \(x \in X\). Let \(f(x)\) be the pointwise limit of the subsequence \(f_{n_k}(x)\) (set \(f(x) = 0\) where it diverges). To show \(f_{n_k} \to f\) in \(L^p\), apply Fatou's Lemma with respect to \(j \to \infty\):
\[ \int_X |f_{n_k} - f|^p \, d\mu = \int_X \liminf_{j\to\infty} |f_{n_k} - f_{n_j}|^p \, d\mu \le \liminf_{j\to\infty} \int_X |f_{n_k} - f_{n_j}|^p \, d\mu. \]
Given \(\varepsilon > 0\), choose \(N\) such that for all \(k, j \ge N\), \(\|f_{n_k} - f_{n_j}\|_p \le \varepsilon\). Then for \(n_k \ge N\), we have \(\|f_{n_k} - f\|_p \le \varepsilon\). Thus \(f - f_{n_k} \in L^p\), so \(f \in L^p\), and \(\|f_{n_k} - f\|_p \to 0\). Since the original sequence is Cauchy and has a convergent subsequence, the entire sequence converges to \(f\) in \(L^p(X, \mu)\).

Case 2: \(p = \infty\). Let \((f_n)\) be a Cauchy sequence in \(L^\infty(X, \mu)\). For each \(n, m \ge 1\), let \(E_{n,m} = \{ x \in X : |f_n(x) - f_m(x)| > \|f_n - f_m\|_\infty \}\). By definition of the essential supremum, \(\mu(E_{n,m}) = 0\). Let \(E = \bigcup_{n,m=1}^\infty E_{n,m}\). Since \(E\) is a countable union of null sets, \(\mu(E) = 0\). For any \(x \notin E\), we have \(|f_n(x) - f_m(x)| \le \|f_n - f_m\|_\infty\) for all \(n, m \ge 1\). This means that for every \(x \notin E\), \((f_n(x))\) is a Cauchy sequence of scalars. Let \(f(x) = \lim_{n\to\infty} f_n(x)\) for \(x \notin E\), and \(f(x) = 0\) for \(x \in E\). Given \(\varepsilon > 0\), there is \(N\) such that for all \(n, m \ge N\), \(\|f_n - f_m\|_\infty \le \varepsilon\). Thus for all \(x \notin E\) and \(n, m \ge N\), \(|f_n(x) - f_m(x)| \le \varepsilon\). Taking \(m \to \infty\) yields \(|f_n(x) - f(x)| \le \varepsilon\) for all \(x \notin E\) and \(n \ge N\). This implies \(\|f_n - f\|_\infty \le \varepsilon\) for all \(n \ge N\), showing \(f_n \to f\) in \(L^\infty(X, \mu)\).
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Show the inclusion of \(L^p\) spaces. Which hypothesis is essential? Justify the answer.
questions.strategyHtml: The proof depends on finite measure. Use Holder with the constant function \(1\) to compare norms, then show the hypothesis is essential by giving an infinite-measure counterexample.
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ \|f\|_p \le \mu(X)^{1/p - 1/q} \|f\|_q. \]
\[ \int_X |f|^p \cdot 1 \, d\mu \le \left( \int_X (|f|^p)^{q/p} \, d\mu \right)^{p/q} \left( \int_X 1^{\frac{q}{q-p}} \, d\mu \right)^{1 - p/q} = \|f\|_q^p \cdot \mu(X)^{1 - p/q}. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Lp inclusions depend on finite measure; interpolation comes from splitting exponents and using Holder.

Theorem: If \(\mu(X) < \infty\) and \(1 \le p \le q \le \infty\), then \(L^q(X, \mu) \subset L^p(X, \mu)\), and:

\[ \|f\|_p \le \mu(X)^{1/p - 1/q} \|f\|_q. \]
Proof: If \(q = \infty\), then \(|f(x)| \le \|f\|_\infty\) a.e., which implies \(\|f\|_p = \left( \int_X |f|^p \, d\mu \right)^{1/p} \le \mu(X)^{1/p} \|f\|_\infty\). If \(q < \infty\), apply Hölder's inequality to the functions \(|f|^p\) and \(1\) with exponents \(r = q/p \ge 1\) and conjugate exponent \(s = \frac{r}{r-1} = \frac{q}{q-p}\):
\[ \int_X |f|^p \cdot 1 \, d\mu \le \left( \int_X (|f|^p)^{q/p} \, d\mu \right)^{p/q} \left( \int_X 1^{\frac{q}{q-p}} \, d\mu \right)^{1 - p/q} = \|f\|_q^p \cdot \mu(X)^{1 - p/q}. \]
Taking the \(1/p\)-th power of both sides yields \(\|f\|_p \le \mu(X)^{1/p - 1/q} \|f\|_q\). The finite-measure hypothesis \(\mu(X) < \infty\) is essential. On infinite-measure spaces, there are no general inclusions. For example, on \(X = (0, \infty)\) with Lebesgue measure, \(f(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{x}} \chi_{(0,1)}(x)\) is in \(L^1\) but not in \(L^2\), while \(g(x) = \frac{1}{x} \chi_{(1,\infty)}(x)\) is in \(L^2\) but not in \(L^1\).
questions.finalReason: not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: State and prove the interpolation inequality.
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ \|f\|_r \le \|f\|_p^\theta \|f\|_q^{1-\theta}. \]
\[ \int_X |f|^r \, d\mu \le \left( \int_X |f|^{\theta r u} \, d\mu \right)^{1/u} \left( \int_X |f|^{(1-\theta)r v} \, d\mu \right)^{1/v} = \left( \int_X |f|^p \, d\mu \right)^{\frac{\theta r}{p}} \left( \int_X |f|^q \, d\mu \right)^{\frac{(1-\theta)r}{q}}. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Interpolation splits |f|^r into two powers and applies Holder with exponents chosen from 1/r = alpha/p + (1-alpha)/q.

Let \((X,\mathcal A,\mu)\) be a measure space, and let \(1\le p

\[ \frac1r=\frac{\alpha}{p}+\frac{1-\alpha}{q}, \]

then

\[ \|f\|_r\le\|f\|_p^\alpha\|f\|_q^{1-\alpha}. \]

Assume first \(q<\infty\). Write

\[ \int_X|f|^r d\mu=\int_X |f|^{\alpha r}|f|^{(1-\alpha)r}d\mu. \]

Apply Holder with conjugate exponents \(P=p/(\alpha r)\) and \(Q=q/((1-\alpha)r)\). They are conjugate precisely because \(\alpha/p+(1-\alpha)/q=1/r\). Hence

\[ \int_X|f|^r d\mu\le \left(\int_X|f|^p d\mu\right)^{\alpha r/p} \left(\int_X|f|^q d\mu\right)^{(1-\alpha)r/q}. \]

Taking the power \(1/r\) gives the result. If \(q=\infty\), then \(\alpha=p/r\), and

\[ \int_X|f|^r d\mu\le\|f\|_\infty^{r-p}\int_X|f|^p d\mu, \]

which gives the same inequality after taking the \(1/r\)-power.
questions.finalReason: not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: How are \(\ell^p\) and \(L^p\) related?
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: For sequences, larger p gives a larger space: ell^p is contained in ell^q when p < q.

The sequence space \(\ell^p\) is a special case of \(L^p(X, \mathcal{A}, \mu)\) where the underlying measure space is \(X = \mathbb{N}\), \(\mathcal{A} = \mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})\) is the discrete \(\sigma\)-algebra, and \(\mu = \#\) is the counting measure. A sequence \(x = (x_k)_{k=1}^\infty\) is a function on \(\mathbb{N}\), and its \(L^p\) integral is exactly the sum \(\sum_{k=1}^\infty |x_k|^p\).

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: What is the inclusion of \(\ell^p\) spaces?
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Lp inclusions depend on finite measure; interpolation comes from splitting exponents and using Holder.

For sequence spaces, if \(1 \le p \le q \le \infty\), then \(\ell^p \subset \ell^q\), and:

\[ \|x\|_q \le \|x\|_p. \]
Proof: If \(x = 0\), the inequality is trivial. If \(x \neq 0\), assume \(\|x\|_p = 1\) by scaling. Then \(\sum_{k=1}^\infty |x_k|^p = 1\), which implies \(|x_k| \le 1\) for all \(k \ge 1\). Since \(q \ge p\), we have \(|x_k|^q \le |x_k|^p\) for all \(k\). Thus \(\sum_{k=1}^\infty |x_k|^q \le \sum_{k=1}^\infty |x_k|^p = 1\). Taking the \(1/q\)-th power yields \(\|x\|_q \le 1 = \|x\|_p\). By scaling, \(\|x\|_q \le \|x\|_p\) holds generally. This inclusion direction is opposite to that of \(L^p\) spaces on finite measure spaces because counting measure has infinite total mass, but its atoms have a positive lower bound (measure 1), forcing sequences to decay to 0 and thus remain bounded.
questions.finalReason: not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: For which \(p\) are \(L^p(\mathbb R)\) and \(\ell^p\) separable?
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Lp is separable for finite p via simple functions; L∞ is the standard nonseparable exception.

Both \(L^p(\mathbb{R})\) and \(\ell^p\) are separable for \(1 \le p < \infty\). - In \(\ell^p\), the set of sequences with finite support and rational values is countable and dense. - In \(L^p(\mathbb{R})\), the set of simple functions of the form \(\sum_{j=1}^m q_j \chi_{I_j}\) where \(q_j \in \mathbb{Q}\) and \(I_j\) are intervals with rational endpoints is countable and dense. The spaces \(L^\infty(\mathbb{R})\) and \(\ell^\infty\) are not separable. - Proof for \(\ell^\infty\): For any subset \(S \subset \mathbb{N}\), let \(\chi_S \in \ell^\infty\) be its characteristic sequence. The set of all subsets \(\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})\) is uncountable, so there are uncountably many such sequences in \(\ell^\infty\). If \(S_1 \neq S_2\), then \(\|\chi_{S_1} - \chi_{S_2}\|_\infty = 1\). Suppose for contradiction that \(\ell^\infty\) is separable, containing a countable dense subset \(D\). For each \(S \subset \mathbb{N}\), the open ball \(B(\chi_S, 1/3)\) must contain an element of \(D\). Since these balls are pairwise disjoint, this defines an injection from the uncountable set \(\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})\) into the countable set \(D\), which is a contradiction.
questions.finalReason: not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Give examples of normed spaces and define the metric induced by a norm.
questions.answerHtml:
Examples of normed spaces include \((\mathbb R^n,\|\cdot\|_p)\), \(C^0([a,b])\) with \(\|f\|_\infty\), \(L^p(X)\), and \(\ell^p\). A norm \(\|\cdot\|\) induces the metric

\[ d(x,y)=\|x-y\|. \]
In this metric, \(x_n\to x\) means \(\|x_n-x\|\to0\), and \((x_n)\) is Cauchy if \(\|x_n-x_m\|\to0\) as \(n,m\to\infty\).

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Define Banach space and state the series criterion for completeness.
questions.strategyHtml: Completeness is often tested through series. Remember the direction: absolute convergence of every series is equivalent to completeness.
questions.answerHtml:
A Banach space is a complete normed vector space, meaning every Cauchy sequence converges in norm to an element of the space. A series \(\sum_n x_n\) in a normed space is convergent if the partial sums \(s_N=\sum_{n=1}^N x_n\) converge in norm.

The useful completeness criterion is: a normed space \(X\) is Banach if every absolutely convergent series converges, i.e.

\[ \sum_{n=1}^\infty \|x_n\|<\infty\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \sum_{n=1}^\infty x_n \text{ converges in }X. \]
If \(X\) is complete, the partial sums are Cauchy because the tails are bounded by \(\sum_{k=m}^n\|x_k\|\), hence converge. Conversely, if every absolutely convergent series converges, take any Cauchy sequence \((y_n)\), extract a subsequence \((y_{n_k})\) with \(\|y_{n_{k+1}}-y_{n_k}\|\le2^{-k}\), and write it as a convergent telescopic series. The subsequence converges, and since the original sequence is Cauchy, the whole sequence converges.
questions.finalReason: not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: State and prove Riesz's lemma and Riesz's compactness theorem for the unit ball.
questions.strategyHtml: Riesz Lemma creates separated unit vectors away from a closed proper subspace. The compactness theorem uses those separated vectors to kill sequential compactness in infinite dimension.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Riesz lemma builds unit vectors separated from a proper closed subspace; iterating it proves the infinite-dimensional unit ball is not compact.

Riesz Lemma. Let \(X\) be a normed space and let \(E\subsetneq X\) be a closed vector subspace. For every \(\varepsilon\in(0,1)\), there exists \(x\in X\) such that

\[ \|x\|=1, \qquad \operatorname{dist}(x,E)\ge1-\varepsilon. \]

Proof. Choose \(y\in X\setminus E\). Since \(E\) is closed, \(d:=\operatorname{dist}(y,E)>0\). By definition of the infimum, choose \(\eta\in E\) such that

\[ d\le \|y-\eta\|\le {d\over1-\varepsilon}. \]

Set \(x=(y-\eta)/\|y-\eta\|\). Then \(\|x\|=1\). For any \(\xi\in E\), since \(\eta+\xi\|y-\eta\|\in E\),

\[ \|x-\xi\|={1\over\|y-\eta\|}\|y-(\eta+\xi\|y-\eta\|)\|\ge {d\over\|y-\eta\|}\ge1-\varepsilon. \]

Taking the infimum over \(\xi\in E\) gives the result.

Riesz compactness theorem. If \(X\) is infinite-dimensional, then the closed unit ball \(\overline B_1(0)\) is not compact.

Proof. Choose \(x_1\) with \(\|x_1\|=1\), and set \(Y_1=\operatorname{span}\{x_1\}\). Since \(Y_1\) is finite-dimensional, it is closed. Applying Riesz lemma with \(\varepsilon=1/2\), choose \(x_2\) with \(\|x_2\|=1\) and \(\operatorname{dist}(x_2,Y_1)\ge1/2\). Inductively, after choosing \(x_1,\dots,x_n\), set \(Y_n=\operatorname{span}\{x_1,\dots,x_n\}\), which is closed and proper, and choose \(x_{n+1}\) with \(\|x_{n+1}\|=1\) and \(\operatorname{dist}(x_{n+1},Y_n)\ge1/2\).

Then for \(i

\[ \|x_j-x_i\|\ge {1\over2}. \]

Thus \((x_n)\subset\overline B_1(0)\) has no Cauchy subsequence, hence no convergent subsequence. In metric spaces compactness implies sequential compactness, so the closed unit ball is not compact.
questions.finalReason: topic/family score

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Define equivalent norms and state what happens in finite-dimensional spaces.
questions.answerHtml:
Two norms \(\|\cdot\|_a\) and \(\|\cdot\|_b\) on a vector space are equivalent if there are constants \(c,C>0\) such that

\[ c\|x\|_a\le \|x\|_b\le C\|x\|_a\qquad\forall x. \]
Equivalent norms generate the same convergent sequences, open sets, and completeness property. In finite-dimensional vector spaces all norms are equivalent. Also, every finite-dimensional subspace of a normed space is closed; arbitrary infinite-dimensional subspaces need not be closed.

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Write the definitions of linear operator, bounded operator, continuous operator, functional, and dual space.
questions.strategyHtml: These definitions are the vocabulary behind the open mapping, closed graph, and Hahn-Banach questions. The key equivalence is bounded equals continuous for linear maps.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Hahn-Banach is extension without increasing the norm; most corollaries start on span{x0}.

A map \(T:X\to Y\) between vector spaces is linear if \(T(\alpha x+\beta y)=\alpha Tx+\beta Ty\). A linear operator between normed spaces is bounded if there exists \(C\ge0\) such that

\[ \|Tx\|_Y\le C\|x\|_X\qquad\forall x\in X. \]
A linear operator is continuous if it is continuous as a map between metric spaces. For linear maps, continuity everywhere is equivalent to continuity at \(0\), and equivalent to boundedness. A functional is a scalar-valued linear operator \(L:X\to\mathbb K\). The dual space \(X^*\) is the space of continuous linear functionals on \(X\), equipped with
\[ \|L\|_{X^*}=\sup_{\|x\|\le1}|L(x)|. \]
questions.finalReason: not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: State and prove the characterization of bounded linear operators. Define the operator norm.
questions.strategyHtml: For linear maps, everything reduces to behavior at \(0\). Use continuity at \(0\) to get a bound on a small ball, then rescale any vector into that ball.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: For linear maps, bounded at the unit ball, continuous at zero, and globally continuous are equivalent.

For a linear operator \(T:X\to Y\) between normed spaces, the following are equivalent: \(T\) is continuous, \(T\) is continuous at \(0\), and \(T\) is bounded.

Proof: Continuity implies continuity at \(0\). If \(T\) is continuous at \(0\), there is \(\delta>0\) such that \(\|x\|\le\delta\) implies \(\|Tx\|\le1\). For \(x\ne0\), apply this to \(\delta x/\|x\|\) to get \(\|Tx\|\le \delta^{-1}\|x\|\). Thus \(T\) is bounded. Boundedness implies continuity because

\[ \|Tx-Ty\|=\|T(x-y)\|\le C\|x-y\|. \]
The operator norm is
\[ \|T\|=\sup_{\|x\|\le1}\|Tx\|=\sup_{\|x\|=1}\|Tx\|=\sup_{x\ne0}\frac{\|Tx\|}{\|x\|}. \]
questions.finalReason: not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Under which hypotheses is \(L(X,Y)\) a Banach space?
questions.strategyHtml: Completeness of operator spaces is inherited from the target, not the source. Take an operator-norm Cauchy sequence and pass to pointwise limits in \(Y\).
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: L(X,Y) is Banach when the target Y is Banach; completeness is pushed into the codomain.

If \(X\) is a normed space and \(Y\) is a Banach space, then \(L(X,Y)\), the space of bounded linear operators from \(X\) to \(Y\), is Banach with the operator norm.

Proof: Let \((T_n)\) be Cauchy in operator norm. For each fixed \(x\in X\),

\[ \|T_nx-T_mx\|_Y\le \|T_n-T_m\|\,\|x\|_X, \]
so \((T_nx)\) is Cauchy in \(Y\). Since \(Y\) is complete, define \(Tx=\lim_n T_nx\). Linearity follows by passing to the limit in \(T_n(\alpha x+\beta y)=\alpha T_nx+\beta T_ny\). Also, since \((T_n)\) is Cauchy, it is bounded in operator norm; say \(\|T_n\|\le M\). Then \(\|Tx\|\le M\|x\|\), so \(T\in L(X,Y)\). Finally, for fixed \(n\),
\[ \|(T_n-T)x\|\le \limsup_m \|T_n-T_m\|\,\|x\|, \]
hence \(\|T_n-T\|\to0\).
questions.finalReason: not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Write the definition of separable metric space and show that \(C^0([a,b])\) is separable.
questions.strategyHtml: The task wants an explicit countable dense set. Define separability, then approximate continuous functions uniformly by polynomials and restrict to polynomials with rational coefficients.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: For C0 separability, approximate continuous functions uniformly by polynomials with rational coefficients.

A metric space \(X\) is separable if it contains a countable, dense subset. That is, there exists a countable subset \(D \subset X\) such that \(\overline{D} = X\).

We show that the space \(C^0([a, b])\) of continuous, real-valued functions on the compact interval \([a, b]\), equipped with the supremum norm \(\|f\|_\infty = \max_{x \in [a, b]} |f(x)|\), is separable by proving that the set of polynomials with rational coefficients, \(\mathbb{Q}[x]\), is countable and dense in \(C^0([a, b])\).

Countability: Every polynomial \(P \in \mathbb{Q}[x]\) can be written as \(P(x) = \sum_{k=0}^n q_k x^k\) for some \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(q_k \in \mathbb{Q}\). For each fixed \(n\), the set of polynomials of degree at most \(n\) with rational coefficients is in bijection with \(\mathbb{Q}^{n+1}\). Since \(\mathbb{Q}\) is countable, \(\mathbb{Q}^{n+1}\) is countable for every \(n\). The set of all polynomials with rational coefficients is the countable union:

\[ \mathbb{Q}[x] = \bigcup_{n=0}^\infty \mathbb{Q}^{n+1}, \]
which is countable because a countable union of countable sets is countable.

Density: Let \(f \in C^0([a, b])\) and let \(\varepsilon > 0\). By the Weierstrass Approximation Theorem, there exists a polynomial \(P(x) = \sum_{k=0}^n c_k x^k\) with real coefficients \(c_k \in \mathbb{R}\) such that:

\[ \|f - P\|_\infty < \frac{\varepsilon}{2}. \]
Let \(M = \max\{|a|, |b|, 1\}\). Since \(\mathbb{Q}\) is dense in \(\mathbb{R}\), we can choose rational numbers \(q_k \in \mathbb{Q}\) for each \(k = 0, 1, \dots, n\) such that:
\[ |c_k - q_k| < \frac{\varepsilon}{2(n+1)M^n}. \]
Define the rational polynomial \(Q(x) = \sum_{k=0}^n q_k x^k \in \mathbb{Q}[x]\). For any \(x \in [a, b]\):
\[ |P(x) - Q(x)| = \left| \sum_{k=0}^n (c_k - q_k) x^k \right| \le \sum_{k=0}^n |c_k - q_k| |x|^k \le \sum_{k=0}^n \frac{\varepsilon}{2(n+1)M^n} M^n = \frac{\varepsilon}{2}. \]
Taking the supremum over \(x \in [a, b]\) yields \(\|P - Q\|_\infty \le \frac{\varepsilon}{2}\). By the triangle inequality:
\[ \|f - Q\|_\infty \le \|f - P\|_\infty + \|P - Q\|_\infty < \frac{\varepsilon}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon}{2} = \varepsilon. \]
Since for any \(f \in C^0([a, b])\) and any \(\varepsilon > 0\), there exists \(Q \in \mathbb{Q}[x]\) with \(\|f - Q\|_\infty < \varepsilon\), the countable set \(\mathbb{Q}[x]\) is dense in \(C^0([a, b])\). Hence, \(C^0([a, b])\) is separable.
questions.finalReason: July exact history

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: State Baire's theorem in its two standard forms.
questions.answerHtml:
Let \(X\) be a complete metric space. Baire's theorem says that \(X\) cannot be written as a countable union of closed sets with empty interior. Equivalently, if

\[ X=\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty F_n \]
with each \(F_n\) closed, then at least one \(F_n\) has nonempty interior. Another equivalent formulation is that a countable intersection of open dense subsets of a complete metric space is dense. This is the category theorem behind Banach-Steinhaus, the Open Mapping Theorem, and the Closed Graph Theorem.
questions.finalReason: exact Jan/Feb-2026 ID; not observed as exact ID in 2023-25

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: State and prove the Banach-Steinhaus theorem, also called the Uniform Boundedness Principle.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Banach-Steinhaus is Baire plus pointwise boundedness, producing uniform operator bounds.

Banach-Steinhaus theorem. Let \(X\) be a Banach space, let \(Y\) be a normed space, and let \(\mathcal F\subset L(X,Y)\). If \(\mathcal F\) is pointwise bounded, i.e.

\[ \sup_{T\in\mathcal F}\|Tx\|_Y<\infty\qquad\forall x\in X, \]

then it is uniformly bounded:

\[ \sup_{T\in\mathcal F}\|T\|_{L(X,Y)}<\infty. \]

Proof. For \(n\in\mathbb N\), define

\[ C_n=\{x\in X:\|Tx\|_Y\le n\quad\forall T\in\mathcal F\}. \]

Each \(C_n\) is closed because every \(T\) is continuous. Pointwise boundedness gives \(X=\bigcup_n C_n\). Since \(X\) is complete, Baire's theorem implies that some \(C_{n_0}\) has nonempty interior. Hence there are \(x_0\in X\) and \(\varepsilon>0\) such that \(\overline B_\varepsilon(x_0)\subset C_{n_0}\).

If \(\|z\|_X\le\varepsilon\), then \(x_0+z\in C_{n_0}\) and \(x_0\in C_{n_0}\), so for every \(T\in\mathcal F\),

\[ \|Tz\|_Y=\|T(x_0+z)-Tx_0\|_Y\le2n_0. \]

For arbitrary \(x\ne0\), apply this to \(z=\varepsilon x/\|x\|\). Then

\[ \|Tx\|_Y\le \frac{2n_0}{\varepsilon}\|x\|_X. \]

Taking the supremum over \(\|x\|\le1\) and over \(T\in\mathcal F\) gives uniform boundedness.
questions.finalReason: exact Jan/Feb-2026 ID; July exact history; repeated core ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.bankRefFull: Theory Questions.pdf: Q2.33, Q2.34, Q2.36, Q2.37
questions.questionHtml: Define open mapping, state the Open Mapping Theorem, state the Bounded Inverse Theorem, and state the Closed Graph Theorem.
questions.answerHtml: A map between topological spaces is open if the image of every open set is open. - Open Mapping Theorem: Let \(X, Y\) be Banach spaces. If \(T \in L(X, Y)\) is surjective, then \(T\) is an open mapping. - Bounded Inverse Theorem: Let \(X, Y\) be Banach spaces. If \(T \in L(X, Y)\) is bijective, then its inverse \(T^{-1} : Y \to X\) is bounded (i.e. continuous). - Closed Graph Theorem: Let \(X, Y\) be Banach spaces. If \(T : X \to Y\) is a linear operator whose graph \(G(T) = \{(x, Tx) : x \in X\}\) is closed in the product space \(X \times Y\) (equipped with the norm \(\|(x,y)\| = \|x\|_X + \|y\|_Y\)), then \(T\) is bounded.
questions.finalReason: exact Jan/Feb-2026 ID; July exact history; repeated core ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Write the definitions of graph and closed operator, and explain the relation with the Closed Graph Theorem.
questions.answerHtml:
For a linear operator \(T:D(T)\subset X\to Y\), its graph is

\[ G(T)=\{(x,Tx):x\in D(T)\}\subset X\times Y. \]
The operator \(T\) is closed if whenever \(x_n\in D(T)\), \(x_n\to x\) in \(X\), and \(Tx_n\to y\) in \(Y\), then \(x\in D(T)\) and \(Tx=y\). Equivalently, \(G(T)\) is closed in \(X\times Y\). The Closed Graph Theorem says that if \(X,Y\) are Banach spaces, \(D(T)=X\), and \(T:X\to Y\) is linear with closed graph, then \(T\) is bounded.

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Define the evaluation map, also called the canonical map, list its main properties, and give the definition of reflexive Banach space.
questions.answerHtml:
Let \(X\) be a normed space. The canonical evaluation map is the mapping \(\tau : X \to X^{**}\) defined by:

\[ \tau(x)(L) = L(x) \qquad \forall L \in X^*. \]
Main Properties: - \(\tau\) is linear: \(\tau(\alpha x + \beta y)(L) = L(\alpha x + \beta y) = \alpha L(x) + \beta L(y) = \alpha \tau(x)(L) + \beta \tau(y)(L)\). - \(\tau\) is an isometry: \(\|\tau(x)\|_{X^{**}} = \|x\|_X\). Indeed, by definition, \(\|\tau(x)\|_{X^{**}} = \sup_{\|L\|_{X^*} \le 1} |L(x)|\). By a corollary of the Hahn-Banach theorem, there exists \(L \in X^*\) with \(\|L\|_{X^*} = 1\) and \(L(x) = \|x\|_X\). Thus, the supremum is exactly \(\|x\|_X\). A Banach space \(X\) is reflexive if the canonical evaluation map \(\tau : X \to X^{**}\) is surjective (and thus is an isometric isomorphism between \(X\) and \(X^{**}\)).
questions.finalReason: exact Jun-2026 ID; repeated core ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: State and prove the Riesz representation theorem in Hilbert spaces.
questions.strategyHtml: Separate the zero functional from the nonzero case. For a nonzero functional, decompose the Hilbert space into kernel plus its orthogonal direction, choose the representing vector on that direction, and verify uniqueness by testing inner products.
questions.keyStepsHtml:
\[ F(x) = \langle x, y \rangle \qquad \forall x \in H. \]
\[ u = x - \frac{F(x)}{F(z_0)} z_0. \]
questions.answerHtml:
Riesz Representation Theorem: Let \(H\) be a Hilbert space. For every continuous linear functional \(F \in H^*\), there exists a unique \(y \in H\) such that:

\[ F(x) = \langle x, y \rangle \qquad \forall x \in H. \]
Furthermore, we have the norm identity \(\|F\|_{H^*} = \|y\|_H\).

Proof: Uniqueness: Suppose there exist \(y_1, y_2 \in H\) such that \(F(x) = \langle x, y_1 \rangle = \langle x, y_2 \rangle\) for all \(x \in H\). Then \(\langle x, y_1 - y_2 \rangle = 0\) for all \(x \in H\). Choosing \(x = y_1 - y_2\) yields \(\|y_1 - y_2\|_H^2 = 0 \implies y_1 = y_2\).

Existence: If \(F = 0\), we choose \(y = 0\). Assume \(F \neq 0\). Let \(M = \ker F = \{x \in H : F(x) = 0\}\). Since \(F\) is continuous and linear, \(M\) is a closed proper subspace of \(H\). By the Projection Theorem Corollary, \(H = M \oplus M^\perp\). Since \(M\) is a proper subspace, \(M^\perp \neq \{0\}\). Choose a non-zero vector \(z_0 \in M^\perp\). Since \(z_0 \notin M\), we have \(F(z_0) \neq 0\). For any \(x \in H\), define the vector:

\[ u = x - \frac{F(x)}{F(z_0)} z_0. \]
By linearity, \(F(u) = F(x) - \frac{F(x)}{F(z_0)} F(z_0) = 0\), so \(u \in M\). Since \(z_0 \in M^\perp\), \(u\) is orthogonal to \(z_0\):
\[ 0 = \langle u, z_0 \rangle = \left\langle x - \frac{F(x)}{F(z_0)} z_0, z_0 \right\rangle = \langle x, z_0 \rangle - \frac{F(x)}{F(z_0)} \|z_0\|_H^2. \]
Solving for \(F(x)\) gives:
\[ F(x) = \left\langle x, \frac{\overline{F(z_0)}}{\|z_0\|_H^2} z_0 \right\rangle. \]
Thus, choosing \(y = \frac{\overline{F(z_0)}}{\|z_0\|_H^2} z_0 \in H\) gives \(F(x) = \langle x, y \rangle\) for all \(x \in H\).

Norm Identity: If \(y = 0\), then \(F = 0\) and \(\|F\|_{H^*} = 0 = \|y\|_H\). If \(y \neq 0\), then by Cauchy-Schwarz:

\[ |F(x)| = |\langle x, y \rangle| \le \|x\|_H \|y\|_H \implies \|F\|_{H^*} = \sup_{x \neq 0} \frac{|F(x)|}{\|x\|_H} \le \|y\|_H. \]
On the other hand, evaluating the functional at \(x = y\) gives:
\[ |F(y)| = \langle y, y \rangle = \|y\|_H^2 \implies \|F\|_{H^*} \ge \frac{|F(y)|}{\|y\|_H} = \|y\|_H. \]
Combining these inequalities yields \(\|F\|_{H^*} = \|y\|_H\).
questions.finalReason: exact Jan/Feb-2026 ID

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Exhibit an example ofT∈ (Lp)∗. Compute∥T∥∗.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Pick \(g\in L^q\), define \(L_g(f)=\int fg\), use Hölder for \(\le\), and test with \(|g|^{q-2}g\) for equality.

Let \((X,\mathcal A,\mu)\) be a measure space, let \(1

\[ L_g:L^p(X)\to\mathbb R,\qquad L_g(f):=\int_X f g\,d\mu. \]

The map is linear by linearity of the integral. By Hölder's inequality,

\[ |L_g(f)|\le\int_X |fg|\,d\mu\le \|f\|_p\|g\|_q. \]

Thus \(L_g\in (L^p)^*\) and \(\|L_g\|_*\le\|g\|_q\).

If \(g=0\) a.e., equality is clear. Otherwise set

\[ \varphi:=\frac{|g|^{q-2}g}{\|g\|_q^{q-1}}. \]

Then \(\varphi\in L^p\), because \((q-1)p=q\), and

\[ \|\varphi\|_p^p={1\over\|g\|_q^q}\int_X |g|^q\,d\mu=1. \]

Moreover,

\[ L_g(\varphi)=\frac{1}{\|g\|_q^{q-1}}\int_X |g|^q\,d\mu=\|g\|_q. \]

Therefore \(\|L_g\|_*\ge\|g\|_q\), and hence

\[ \|L_g\|_* = \|g\|_q. \]

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Let (V,⟨·,·⟩) be a ﬁnite dimensional vector space endowed with an inner product. Characterize V∗.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Finite-dimensional Riesz: choose an orthonormal basis and represent L by the vector of its values on that basis.

Let \(V\) be a finite-dimensional real inner-product space. Then every \(L\in V^*\) is represented uniquely by a vector \(y\in V\):

\[ L(x)=\langle x,y\rangle\qquad\forall x\in V, \]

and \(\|L\|_{V^*}=\|y\|_V\). Thus \(V^*\) is isometrically isomorphic to \(V\).

Choose an orthonormal basis \(\{v_1,\dots,v_n\}\). Every \(x\in V\) can be written as \(x=\sum_i \langle x,v_i\rangle v_i\). Hence

\[ L(x)=\sum_i\langle x,v_i\rangle L(v_i)=\left\langle x,\sum_i L(v_i)v_i\right\rangle. \]

Set \(y=\sum_iL(v_i)v_i\). This proves existence. If also \(L(x)=\langle x,z\rangle\), then \(\langle x,y-z\rangle=0\) for all \(x\); taking \(x=y-z\) gives \(y=z\). Finally, Cauchy-Schwarz gives \(|L(x)|\le\|x\|\|y\|\), so \(\|L\|\le\|y\|\); evaluating at \(x=y/\|y\|\) when \(y\ne0\) gives equality.

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: In a metric space, write the deﬁnitions of: bounded set, closed set, compact set, sequentially compact set. Explain how all these properties are related.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: In metric spaces, compact equals sequentially compact; compact implies closed and bounded, but not conversely in infinite dimension.

Let \((X,d)\) be a metric space and let \(E\subset X\).

\(E\) is bounded if there exist \(x_0\in X\) and \(R>0\) such that \(d(x,x_0)\le R\) for all \(x\in E\).

\(E\) is closed if it contains the limits of its convergent sequences: whenever \((x_n)\subset E\) and \(x_n\to x\in X\), then \(x\in E\).

\(E\) is compact if every open cover of \(E\) admits a finite subcover.

\(E\) is sequentially compact if every sequence in \(E\) has a subsequence converging to a point of \(E\).

In metric spaces, compactness and sequential compactness are equivalent. Compact sets are always closed and bounded. The converse is false in general: in an infinite-dimensional normed space, the closed unit ball is closed and bounded but not compact by Riesz's compactness theorem. In \(\mathbb R^N\), however, Heine-Borel says that compact is equivalent to closed and bounded.

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Write the deﬁnitions ofLp and Lp. Show that Lp is a vector space (and its preliminary lemma).
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Lp is first a function class, then Lp is the quotient by equality a.e.; vector-space closure uses (a+b)^p <= 2^{p-1}(a^p+b^p).

For \(1\le p<\infty\), define

\[ \mathcal L^p(X):=\left\{f\in M(X,\mathcal A):\int_X|f|^p\,d\mu<\infty\right\}. \]

On \(\mathcal L^p\), define \(f\sim g\) if \(f=g\) a.e. Then

\[ L^p(X):=\mathcal L^p(X)/\sim. \]

The preliminary estimate is: for \(a,b\ge0\),

\[ (a+b)^p\le2^{p-1}(a^p+b^p). \]

This follows from convexity of \(t\mapsto t^p\): \(((a+b)/2)^p\le(a^p+b^p)/2\).

To show that \(\mathcal L^p\) is a vector space, take \(f,g\in\mathcal L^p\) and \(\lambda\in\mathbb R\). The function \(f+\lambda g\) is measurable and finite a.e. Moreover,

\[ |f+\lambda g|^p\le(|f|+|\lambda||g|)^p\le2^{p-1}|f|^p+2^{p-1}|\lambda|^p|g|^p. \]

The right-hand side is integrable, hence \(f+\lambda g\in\mathcal L^p\). Passing to equivalence classes gives that \(L^p\) is a vector space.

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Characterize Lebesgue measurability of functions and prove it.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Lebesgue measurability can be tested on open sets, or for real functions on any one family of level sets.

Let \((X,\mathcal L)\) be a measurable space and let \((Y,d)\) be a metric space with Borel \(\sigma\)-algebra \(\mathcal B(Y)\). A function \(f:X\to Y\) is Lebesgue measurable if and only if

\[ f^{-1}(G)\in\mathcal L\qquad\text{for every open }G\subset Y. \]

Indeed, open sets generate \(\mathcal B(Y)\), and inverse images commute with complements and countable unions. Hence checking inverse images on a generating class is enough.

For \(f:X\to\overline{\mathbb R}\), this is equivalent to any one of the following conditions, for every \(\alpha\in\mathbb R\):

\[ \{f>\alpha\}\in\mathcal L,\quad \{f\ge\alpha\}\in\mathcal L,\quad \{f<\alpha\}\in\mathcal L,\quad \{f\le\alpha\}\in\mathcal L. \]

For example, the rays \((\alpha,+\infty]\) generate the Borel sets of \(\overline{\mathbb R}\), so measurability is equivalent to \(\{f>\alpha\}\in\mathcal L\) for all \(\alpha\). The other forms follow by complements and countable unions/intersections, e.g.

\[ \{f<\alpha\}=\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty\{f\le\alpha-1/n\},\qquad \{f>\alpha\}=\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty\{f\ge\alpha+1/n\}. \]
