questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Let f,g ∈M (X,A). Is it true thatf +g,fg ∈ (X,A)?
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: For finite-valued measurable functions, algebra is safe; for extended-valued sums, avoid +infinity minus infinity.

If \(f,g:X\to\mathbb R\) are finite real-valued measurable functions, then \(f+g\) and \(fg\) are measurable. Indeed, the maps \((s,t)\mapsto s+t\) and \((s,t)\mapsto st\) are continuous on \(\mathbb R^2\), and \(x\mapsto(f(x),g(x))\) is measurable.

If \(f,g\) are extended-real-valued, one must be more careful. The sum \(f+g\) is not defined at points where \(+\infty-\infty\) or \(-\infty+\infty\) occurs. Therefore, without excluding those points, it is not even a well-defined extended-real function. If the indeterminate forms do not occur, then the sum is measurable. Products are measurable under the usual extended-real conventions, but one must state the convention being used, especially for \(0\cdot\infty\).

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: State and give a sketch of the proof of the Simple Approximation Theorem.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Approximate by cutting the range into dyadic strips and taking the lower endpoint on each strip.

Simple Approximation Theorem. Let \((X,\mathcal A)\) be a measurable space and let \(f:X\to\overline{\mathbb R}\) be measurable. Then there exists a sequence of simple measurable functions \(s_n\) such that \(s_n\to f\) pointwise. If \(f\ge0\), the sequence can be chosen with

\[ 0\le s_n\le s_{n+1}\le f,\qquad s_n\uparrow f. \]

If \(f\) is bounded, the convergence can be made uniform.

For the core case \(0\le f\le1\), split the range into intervals of length \(2^{-n}\). For \(k=0,\dots,2^n-1\), set

\[ E_k^{(n)}=\left\{x:\frac{k}{2^n}\le f(x)<\frac{k+1}{2^n}\right\},\qquad s_n=\sum_{k=0}^{2^n-1}\frac{k}{2^n}\chi_{E_k^{(n)}}. \]

Since \(f\) is measurable, the sets \(E_k^{(n)}\) are measurable, hence \(s_n\) is simple and measurable. Also

\[ 0\le f(x)-s_n(x)<2^{-n}, \]

so \(s_n\to f\) uniformly in this bounded case. For general nonnegative \(f\), apply the same construction to truncations \(f\wedge n\). For a general real-valued measurable function, apply the construction to \(f^+\) and \(f^-\), and subtract.

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: Write the deﬁnitions ofesssupXf and essinf Xf. State their properties and prove some of them.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Essential bounds ignore null sets: choose one null set outside which the bound is true.

Let \((X,\mathcal A,\mu)\) be a measure space and let \(f:X\to\overline{\mathbb R}\). Denote by \(\mathcal N_\mu\) the family of \(\mu\)-null sets. Define

\[ \operatorname*{esssup}_X f:=\inf_{N\in\mathcal N_\mu}\sup_{x\in N^c}f(x),\qquad \operatorname*{essinf}_X f:=\sup_{N\in\mathcal N_\mu}\inf_{x\in N^c}f(x). \]

Main properties:

  1. \(f\le \operatorname*{esssup}_Xf\) a.e.; similarly \(f\ge \operatorname*{essinf}_Xf\) a.e.
  2. \(\operatorname*{esssup}_Xf=-\operatorname*{essinf}_X(-f)\).
  3. If \(k\ge0\), then \(\operatorname*{esssup}_X(kf)=k\operatorname*{esssup}_Xf\).
  4. If \(f\le g\) a.e., then \(\operatorname*{esssup}_Xf\le\operatorname*{esssup}_Xg\).
  5. \(\operatorname*{esssup}_X(f+g)\le\operatorname*{esssup}_Xf+\operatorname*{esssup}_Xg\).
  6. If \(f=g\) a.e., then their essential suprema and infima coincide.

For example, let \(\alpha=\operatorname*{esssup}_Xf<+\infty\). For every \(n\) choose a null set \(N_n\) such that \(\sup_{N_n^c}f<\alpha+1/n\). Then \(N=\bigcup_nN_n\) is null and for \(x\in N^c\), \(f(x)\le\alpha\). This proves \(f\le\operatorname*{esssup}f\) a.e. The monotonicity property follows by combining this with the a.e. inequality \(f\le g\).

questions.subject: measure
questions.questionHtml: What isL∞? Which is the relation between functions ﬁnite a.e. and essentially bounded functions? Justify the answer.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: \(L^\infty\) means essentially bounded, which implies finite a.e.; finite a.e. alone does not control blow-up.

Let \((X,\mathcal A,\mu)\) be a measure space. A measurable function \(f:X\to\mathbb R\) is essentially bounded if

\[ \operatorname*{ess\,sup}_X |f|<\infty. \]

The space \(L^\infty(X,\mathcal A,\mu)\) is the quotient, modulo equality a.e., of the essentially bounded measurable functions, with norm

\[ \|f\|_\infty:=\operatorname*{ess\,sup}_X |f|. \]

Every \(f\in L^\infty\) is finite a.e.: if \(M=\operatorname*{ess\,sup}_X|f|<\infty\), then \(|f(x)|\le M\) for a.e. \(x\), so \(|f|=\infty\) only on a null set.

The converse is false. On \((\mathbb R,\mathcal L,\lambda)\), define

\[ f(x)=\begin{cases}|x|^{-1},&x\ne0,\\ +\infty,&x=0.\end{cases} \]

The function is finite a.e. because \(\lambda(\{0\})=0\), but it is not essentially bounded: near \(0\), \(|f|\) is arbitrarily large on sets of positive measure. Hence \(\operatorname*{ess\,sup}_{\mathbb R}|f|=+\infty\), so \(f\notin L^\infty\).

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Write the deﬁnitions of Cauchy sequence and convergent sequence in a metric space. What is a complete metric space?
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Convergent means close to one limit; Cauchy means terms are close to each other; complete means Cauchy sequences have limits inside the space.

Let \((X,d)\) be a metric space.

A sequence \((x_n)\subset X\) converges to \(x\in X\), written \(x_n\to x\), if for every \(\varepsilon>0\) there exists \(\bar n\in\mathbb N\) such that

\[ n>\bar n\quad\Longrightarrow\quad d(x_n,x)<\varepsilon. \]

A sequence \((x_n)\subset X\) is Cauchy if for every \(\varepsilon>0\) there exists \(\bar n\in\mathbb N\) such that

\[ n,m>\bar n\quad\Longrightarrow\quad d(x_n,x_m)<\varepsilon. \]

The metric space \((X,d)\) is complete if every Cauchy sequence in \(X\) converges to an element of \(X\). Every convergent sequence is Cauchy, but the converse is exactly the completeness property.

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: From the UBP it is possibile to infer an important property of operators deﬁned by means of a point-wise limit. What is that? Justify your answer.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: A pointwise limit of bounded linear operators is bounded because UBP first gives a uniform operator-norm bound.

Let \(X\) be Banach, let \(Y\) be normed, and let \((T_n)\subset L(X,Y)\). Suppose that for every \(x\in X\) the limit

\[ T(x):=\lim_{n\to\infty}T_nx \]

exists in \(Y\). Then \(T\) is linear, and \(T\in L(X,Y)\).

Linearity follows by passing to the limit in \(T_n(\alpha x+\beta y)=\alpha T_nx+\beta T_ny\). For boundedness, fix \(x\in X\). Since \(T_nx\) converges, the set \(\{T_nx:n\in\mathbb N\}\) is bounded. Thus the family \(\{T_n\}\) is pointwise bounded. By Banach-Steinhaus, there exists \(M>0\) such that \(\|T_n\|\le M\) for all \(n\). Hence

\[ \|T(x)\|_Y=\lim_n\|T_nx\|_Y\le M\|x\|_X. \]

Therefore \(T\) is bounded and \(\|T\|\le M\).

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Write the deﬁnition of pre-Hilbert and of Hilbert spaces. Write the parallelogram law.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Hilbert equals complete inner-product space; parallelogram law is the norm identity coming from the scalar product.

A pre-Hilbert space is a vector space \(H\) endowed with an inner product \(\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle\). The inner product induces the norm

\[ \|x\|:=\sqrt{\langle x,x\rangle}. \]

A Hilbert space is a pre-Hilbert space that is complete for the metric induced by this norm:

\[ d(x,y):=\|x-y\|. \]

The parallelogram law states that for all \(a,b\in H\),

\[ \left\|{a+b\over2}\right\|^2+\left\|{a-b\over2}\right\|^2={1\over2}\bigl(\|a\|^2+\|b\|^2\bigr). \]

Equivalently, \(\|a+b\|^2+\|a-b\|^2=2\|a\|^2+2\|b\|^2\). A norm satisfying the parallelogram identity is induced by an inner product.

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Write the theorem about the abstract Fourier series in Hilbert spaces. Write Par- seval’s identity.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: In a Hilbert basis, every vector equals its Fourier series and the squared norm equals the sum of squared coefficients.

Let \((e_k)_{k\ge1}\) be an orthonormal basis of a Hilbert space \(H\). For every \(u\in H\), the abstract Fourier series of \(u\) is

\[ u=\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\langle u,e_k\rangle e_k, \]

where the convergence is strong in \(H\), i.e. in norm. The numbers \(\langle u,e_k\rangle\) are the Fourier coefficients of \(u\).

Parseval's identity states that

\[ \|u\|^2=\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}|\langle u,e_k\rangle|^2. \]

Equivalently, the partial sums \(S_Nu:=\sum_{k=1}^N\langle u,e_k\rangle e_k\) satisfy \(S_Nu\to u\) in \(H\), and the norm of \(u\) is completely recovered from its Fourier coefficients.

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: Write the deﬁnition of orthonormal basis of a Hilbert space. Exhibit some examples.
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: Orthonormal basis means orthonormal plus dense span, not just orthogonal vectors.

Let \(H\) be a Hilbert space. A sequence \((e_n)\subset H\) is an orthonormal basis, or Hilbert basis, if:

1. it is orthonormal:

\[ \langle e_n,e_m\rangle=0\quad(n\ne m), \qquad \|e_n\|=1\quad\forall n; \]

2. its linear span is dense in \(H\):

\[ \overline{\operatorname{span}\{e_n:n\ge1\}}=H. \]

Equivalently, for every \(u\in H\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exist \(N\in\mathbb N\) and scalars \(\alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_N\) such that

\[ \left\|u-\sum_{k=1}^N\alpha_k e_k\right\|<\varepsilon. \]

Examples include the canonical basis of \(\ell^2\), and the usual trigonometric bases in \(L^2\)-type spaces, such as sine and cosine systems arising from Fourier series.

questions.subject: functional
questions.questionHtml: What is it possible to say about convergence of an o.n.b.?
questions.answerHtml:
Memory cue: An orthonormal basis converges weakly to zero because Fourier coefficients go to zero, but not strongly because norms stay one.

Let \((e_n)\) be an orthonormal basis of a Hilbert space \(H\). Then

\[ e_n\rightharpoonup0, \qquad e_n\not\to0\text{ strongly}. \]

For weak convergence, fix \(f\in H\). By Parseval's identity,

\[ \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}|\langle f,e_n\rangle|^2=\|f\|^2<\infty. \]

Hence the general term tends to zero, so \(\langle f,e_n\rangle\to0\). By the Riesz representation theorem, every \(L\in H^*\) has the form \(L(x)=\langle x,f\rangle\) for some \(f\in H\). Therefore \(L(e_n)\to0\) for every \(L\in H^*\), which means \(e_n\rightharpoonup0\).

Strong convergence to zero would require \(\|e_n\|\to0\). But \(\|e_n\|=1\) for all \(n\). Hence there is no strong convergence to zero.
